messages.content:
You paraphrase a research PROBLEM STATEMENT across 7 ML domains.
Your output is a *domain-grounded problem description* — written in
the target domain's natural research vocabulary, but stating only
the failure mode, NOT a proposed solution.

## Two-step procedure
1. Extract the FAILURE MODE from the input query: the underlying
   gap, bottleneck, or constraint. Strip out any solution direction
   the query may already suggest. Express the failure mode as one
   abstract sentence (the "essence").
2. For each domain, describe an analogous failure mode in that
   domain — using *3-5 domain-specific technical terms* so the
   paraphrase reads like a problem statement an actual researcher
   in that domain would write in their paper's introduction.

## Strict rules — follow exactly
- Use PROBLEM vocabulary: "X is slow when ...", "Y fails when ...",
  "Z is costly because ...", "W is unavailable but needed for ..."
- DO NOT use SOLUTION vocabulary: NO "use ... to ...", NO "build ...
  that ...", NO "apply ... for ...", NO "design a ... which ...".
- DO NOT name a specific algorithmic *solution* (e.g. "two-stage
  cascade", "Gumbel-Softmax sampling"). The paraphrase describes a
  problem; the analogous mechanisms will be discovered downstream.
- DO use domain-specific *concepts and quantities* that bound the
  failure mode (e.g. "candidate pool", "feature map resolution",
  "rollout horizon", "phoneme alignment") — these anchor the
  problem in the domain's natural research vocabulary.
- The paraphrase MUST end as a problem statement, not a solution
  prescription.

## Domain vocabulary anchors (use 3-5 per paraphrase, NOT exhaustive)
  LLM_NLP    : token, decoding step, KV cache, context length,
               attention head, perplexity, in-context examples
  MULTIMODAL : visual token, image embedding, cross-modal alignment,
               query-conditioned attention, caption, VQA accuracy
  CV         : feature map, patch token, spatial resolution,
               receptive field, anchor box, segmentation mask
  RL         : policy, value function, rollout, reward signal,
               trajectory, exploration, sample efficiency
  IR_REC     : candidate pool, query-document score, reranking,
               cross-encoder, recall@k, embedding index
  SPEECH     : acoustic frame, phoneme, spectrogram, alignment,
               WER, streaming latency, vocoder
  ROBOTICS   : sensorimotor loop, manipulation policy, dynamics
               model, motion plan, sim-to-real, contact reasoning

Push hard — even cross-domain mappings that *feel* forced often
surface useful seeds when essence-aligned. Only emit `null` for a
domain when the failure mode genuinely has no analog there.

## Reference example
  Input query: "Sequential LLM decoding is slow because each token
                needs a full large-model forward pass; how can a small
                draft model propose tokens in parallel that the large
                model verifies without quality loss?"

  GOOD essence: "Sequential single-step inference incurs O(N)
                 expensive forward passes when N items must be
                 decided in order, even when many decisions could
                 be made in parallel."

  GOOD IR_REC paraphrase: "In information retrieval, cross-encoder
                            reranking of a candidate pool requires a
                            forward pass per (query, document) pair;
                            recall@k depends on this expensive
                            scoring step yet most candidates could
                            be filtered with cheaper signals before
                            final ranking."
                            ↑ uses: cross-encoder, candidate pool,
                                     query-document scoring, recall@k,
                                     reranking — IR vocabulary

  GOOD CV paraphrase:      "In dense visual perception, per-patch
                            token processing through a heavy encoder
                            dominates inference latency for high
                            spatial resolution inputs; segmentation
                            quality depends on this dense scoring
                            even though most patches are background
                            or low-saliency."
                            ↑ uses: patch token, spatial resolution,
                                     segmentation, dense scoring

  BAD IR_REC paraphrase (DO NOT produce — solution-form):
                          "Build a two-stage ranker where a cheap
                           encoder proposes candidates and a
                           cross-encoder verifies accepted spans."

  BAD IR_REC paraphrase (DO NOT produce — too abstract):
                          "Relevance estimation is sequential and
                           costly when each item must be scored in
                           turn; quality must not degrade under
                           cheaper alternatives."
                           ↑ no IR-specific vocabulary, would
                             match poorly against real IR seeds

Always produce the GOOD form: domain-vocabulary-rich problem
statement.

Output JSON:
  {
    "essence":  "<one sentence stating the abstract failure mode>",
    "paraphrases": {
      "LLM_NLP":    "<domain-grounded problem statement>",
      "MULTIMODAL": "...",
      "CV":         "...",
      "RL":         "...",
      "IR_REC":     "...",
      "SPEECH":     "...",
      "ROBOTICS":   null
    }
  }

Query: Models are brittle to adversarial perturbations imperceptible to
humans; how can pretraining objectives or data augmentation make
models robust without expensive test-time defenses?

You synthesize a method that addresses the user's query by drawing on
a set of retrieved research seeds. Each seed is a published
contribution with a (problem, method, domain, paper_title) tuple, and
it arrives with a "Retrieved via" lens — the text (raw query or a
paraphrase reframing the query for a specific domain) that surfaced
this seed during retrieval. Use the lens as a hint about WHY this
seed was considered; the paraphrase lens often reveals an analogy
between the query and a mechanism family the raw query alone would
not have surfaced. The seeds may come from any subset of ML domains;
use those whose mechanisms genuinely help, and do not force
connections to seeds that do not fit.

Distractor handling: when a seed's lens explicitly indicates it was
NOT retrieved for this query (e.g., "uniform random sample of the
library; no targeted retrieval"), treat that seed as a distractor.
Ignore it unless its mechanism genuinely fits the query for reasons
unrelated to retrieval. It is correct and expected to leave such
seeds out of inspired_by; do not pad inspired_by to make it look
like the distractors were used.

Your output MUST be JSON:
  {
    "method":    "<3–5 paragraphs describing the proposed method end-to-end>",
    "rationale": "<why this addresses the user query, in 1–3 paragraphs>",
    "inspired_by": [
      { "seed_id": "<from one of the provided seeds>",
        "domain":  "<that seed's domain>",
        "borrowed_aspect": "<short noun phrase: what was taken from this seed>" },
      ...
    ]
  }

Provenance: list a seed in inspired_by when its mechanism genuinely
contributed to the method. You MAY include steps that no seed
supports when they are necessary glue logic, problem-specific
adaptation, or a novel addition; do not omit a needed step just
because no seed inspired it. Do not pad inspired_by with seeds that
did not actually contribute.

## Query
Models are brittle to adversarial perturbations imperceptible to
humans; how can pretraining objectives or data augmentation make
models robust without expensive test-time defenses?


## Retrieved seeds

### Seed cc8944ac8c5a [MULTIMODAL] — "AudioGenX: Explainability on Text-to-Audio Generative Models"
- Retrieved via: Autoregressive language models fail when adversarial perturbations to a few prompt tokens within the context length induce large shifts in next-token probabilities and perplexity; robustness that depends on test-time defenses inflates decoding steps and serving latency.
- Problem: Text-to-audio generative models lack faithful, token-level explanations of how each word in the prompt influences the generated audio, and attention-based or single-token perturbation methods miss multi-token interactions and are inefficient over long sequences.
- Method:  AudioGenX learns a soft importance mask over text tokens for each generated audio token by perturbing the cross-attention mechanism and optimizing an Explainer MLP (sigmoid + Gumbel-softmax) to predict the mask. It jointly uses factual and counterfactual objectives on the latent audio-token embedding e_t: maximize cosine similarity under factual masking and minimize it under counterfactual masking, with L1/L2 regularization for sparse, simple masks. Explanations are computed per audio token (parallelizable) and then aggregated over time to provide holistic or interval-specific explanations. The approach supports simultaneous multi-token perturbations and is model-agnostic for TAG models that use cross-attention.

### Seed 7021ab5db5e7 [SPEECH] — "UniCATS: A Unified Context-Aware Text-to-Speech Framework with Contextual VQ-Diffusion and Vocoding"
- Retrieved via: Autoregressive language models fail when adversarial perturbations to a few prompt tokens within the context length induce large shifts in next-token probabilities and perplexity; robustness that depends on test-time defenses inflates decoding steps and serving latency.
- Problem: Autoregressive discrete-token TTS models are constrained to left-to-right generation, making them ill-suited for speech editing where both preceding and following contexts must be respected. Using acoustic tokens further adds RVQ prediction complexity and inherits audio quality limits from codec models.
- Method:  CTX-txt2vec introduces contextual VQ-diffusion to generate semantic tokens conditioned on both sides of context. It concatenates corrupted target tokens with clean preceding and following context [cA, x_t, cB] and feeds them to a Transformer VQ-diffusion decoder, using a binary indicator embedding to distinguish context vs. target. Text is aligned to token length via a duration predictor and length regulator, and the aligned text encoding is injected by addition to self-attention outputs instead of cross-attention to respect strict alignment. Training randomly samples utterances with both/one/no context, while inference rescales predicted durations to match context speed and iteratively denoises from fully masked tokens.

### Seed 631afbec883a [SPEECH] — "End-to-end Contrastive Language-Speech Pretraining Model For Long-form Spoken Question Answering"
- Retrieved via: Autoregressive language models fail when adversarial perturbations to a few prompt tokens within the context length induce large shifts in next-token probabilities and perplexity; robustness that depends on test-time defenses inflates decoding steps and serving latency.
- Problem: Training the non-autoregressive ASR within the retriever is brittle—recognition errors degrade the learned token distributions and downstream retrieval quality; improving the decoder’s context modeling and stability is needed.
- Method:  CLSR introduces a sampler for training: after a first-pass decode, it compares predicted tokens with ground truth, and selectively replaces embeddings of misrecognized tokens by their true text embeddings at a mixing ratio λ to form semantic features for a second decoding pass. This stabilizes token distributions used by the VQ adaptor and improves both WER and retrieval recall. Ablations show that removing the VQ quantizer severely harms retrieval, while enabling the sampler improves ASR and retrieval metrics (e.g., on Spoken-SQuAD, WER decreases and R@k increases).

### Seed 7f79d5d52bbc [MULTIMODAL] — "VQS: Linking Segmentations to Questions and Answers for Supervised Attention in VQA and Question-Focused Semantic Segmentation"
- Retrieved via: Vision–language models fail when imperceptible pixel-level changes alter visual tokens and image embeddings enough to break cross-modal alignment under query-conditioned attention, degrading VQA accuracy; robustness that depends on test-time defenses adds substantial inference latency.
- Problem: Existing VQA systems lack explicit supervision that links questions/answers to the precise image regions that constitute visual evidence, leading to weak or misaligned attentions; related annotations like human attention maps are coarse and bounding boxes often do not directly answer diverse question types.
- Method:  Introduce VQS, a dataset that explicitly links COCO instance segmentations to VQA question–answer pairs by asking annotators to select the segmentation(s) that visually answer each question (with tight boxes when needed). This produces 37,868 images, 96,508 questions, 108,537 segmentations, and 43,725 boxes, enabling transfer of supervision between segmentation and VQA and supporting both supervised attention and new grounded tasks.

### Seed d919f55daf69 [MULTIMODAL] — "ViLT: Vision-and-Language Transformer Without Convolution or Region Supervision"
- Retrieved via: Vision–language models fail when imperceptible pixel-level changes alter visual tokens and image embeddings enough to break cross-modal alignment under query-conditioned attention, degrading VQA accuracy; robustness that depends on test-time defenses adds substantial inference latency.
- Problem: Vision-and-Language Pre-training models typically rely on heavy visual embedders (region detectors or deep CNN backbones), making inference slow and constraining expressiveness to a predefined visual vocabulary.
- Method:  ViLT replaces region/CNN visual features with a shallow, convolution-free linear patch projection and uses a single-stream transformer to jointly process image patches and text tokens. The transformer is initialized from ViT-B/32 pretrained on ImageNet, with image patches (P=32) and text embeddings concatenated with type embeddings and fed through 12 transformer layers. Pretrained with ITM and MLM, this monolithic design yields large efficiency gains (e.g., ~15 ms vs. ~900 ms for region-feature models; ≥4× faster than grid-feature models) while achieving competitive VQAv2, NLVR2, and retrieval performance.

### Seed 359f98a60b38 [MULTIMODAL] — "Multi-Modal Hallucination Control by Visual Information Grounding"
- Retrieved via: Vision–language models fail when imperceptible pixel-level changes alter visual tokens and image embeddings enough to break cross-modal alignment under query-conditioned attention, degrading VQA accuracy; robustness that depends on test-time defenses adds substantial inference latency.
- Problem: Autoregressive vision-language models (VLMs) often hallucinate objects or attributes because the model’s reliance on the visual prompt fades as more tokens are generated, and there is no principled, annotation-free way to quantify this grounding loss.
- Method:  Introduce a visual Prompt Dependency Measure (PDM) that compares the conditioned distribution p(y_t | y_<t, x, c) to the unconditioned language-only distribution p(y_t | y_<t, x) using distances such as the Hellinger metric (PDM-H). This quantifies how much a token depends on the image versus the language prior. Empirically, PDM decreases with token position (conditioning dilution/fading memory), and lower PDM correlates with increased hallucinated objects in captions, while also identifying ‘contextual pressure’ cases (e.g., function words) where low PDM does not imply hallucination.

### Seed 25b7f183d8ad [LLM_NLP] — "Tailor: Generating and Perturbing Text with Semantic Controls"
- Retrieved via: Models are brittle to adversarial perturbations imperceptible to
humans; how can pretraining objectives or data augmentation make
models robust without expensive test-time defenses?

- Problem: Controlled text perturbation for evaluation and robustness typically requires task-specific models or sparse controls that don’t specify how to realize targeted edits, making them costly and hard to generalize.
- Method:  Tailor finetunes a pretrained seq2seq model (T5) to follow structured control codes derived from PropBank SRL, covering three control types: semantic roles, keyword content with specificity (complete/partial/sparse), and span ordering via predicate form and blank positions. Inputs use a bracketed header of controls plus a context with blanks; outputs explicitly tag generated spans, decoupling semantic intent from surface placement and enabling strict or flexible edits by masking subsets of arguments. To enforce adherence to controls, Tailor adds unlikelihood training with negative samples created by perturbing roles, lemmas, and specificities, yielding higher controllability and closeness than MLE alone.

### Seed 4239e7f25909 [CV] — "Overload: Latency Attacks on Object Detection for Edge Devices"
- Retrieved via: Models are brittle to adversarial perturbations imperceptible to
humans; how can pretraining objectives or data augmentation make
models robust without expensive test-time defenses?

- Problem: Existing adversarial attacks on object detectors largely target accuracy, or when targeting latency, rely on complex IoU-based objectives that are computationally and memory intensive—especially problematic on edge devices. The key open question is which model factors most effectively increase inference time with minimal attack cost.
- Method:  Overload analyzes NMS time complexity and shows latency is dominated by the number of objects fed into NMS rather than the number of surviving boxes. Based on this, it formulates a simple white-box objective that maximizes per-detection objectness confidence and class probabilities via a monotonic increasing function, inflating the number of pre-NMS objects without tracking pairwise IoUs. The attack is optimized with PGD-style updates under a small perturbation budget and is NMS-agnostic. Empirically, it generates >20k ghost objects and increases end-to-end latency by ~11–13× on Jetson NX, while using less memory/time than Daedalus and Phantom Sponge (e.g., ~3 min for 16 images at 7.5 GB vs. ~145 min for 10 images at 8.5 GB).

### Seed bd24c7a92f6d [RL] — "Stabilizing Off-Policy Deep Reinforcement Learning from Pixels"
- Retrieved via: Models are brittle to adversarial perturbations imperceptible to
humans; how can pretraining objectives or data augmentation make
models robust without expensive test-time defenses?

- Problem: Off-policy TD-learning from pixel observations is highly unstable: critics quickly overfit to their own noisy targets and fail to learn from sparse, low-magnitude rewards, and it is unclear why image augmentations help.
- Method:  The paper diagnoses a 'visual deadly triad'—exclusive reliance on TD-loss, unregularized CNN encoders, and low-magnitude sparse rewards—showing it induces catastrophic self-overfitting. Using offline policy-evaluation on fixed transitions, the authors demonstrate near-perfect correlation to target Q-values but poor correlation to Monte-Carlo returns without augmentations, and that overfitting is tied to high-frequency, spatially inconsistent CNN features. They introduce the Normalized Discontinuity (ND) score to quantify feature/gradient discontinuities, show ND strongly correlates with performance in online and offline training, and explain random shift augmentations as implicit gradient smoothing through shifted computation graphs.

### Seed ec79896a1fe8 [ROBOTICS] — "Meta Reinforcement Learning for Sim-to-real Domain Adaptation"
- Retrieved via: Policies fail when small adversarial perturbations to observations or dynamics along the rollout induce compounding value-function error and reward-signal collapse; robustness that depends on test-time defenses increases control-loop latency.
- Problem: Policies trained in simulation often fail to transfer to real robots under unknown or varying dynamics; domain-randomized policies can adapt unpredictably and even deteriorate during on-policy updates on hardware.
- Method:  Use a gradient-based meta-reinforcement learning framework (MAML-like) that trains a meta-policy across tasks defined by randomized dynamics, explicitly optimizing expected return after N inner-loop adaptation steps. At deployment, the policy performs K-rollout inner-loop updates with vanilla policy gradient to quickly adapt to the current dynamics, while the outer meta-update backpropagates through the inner steps and is optimized with PPO. Experiments in simulation and on a KUKA puck-hitting task show stable, consistent adaptation and better performance than a domain-randomization baseline.

### Seed dcdea31ed6ae [IR_REC] — "Multi-Task Fusion via Reinforcement Learning for Long-Term User Satisfaction in Recommender Systems"
- Retrieved via: Policies fail when small adversarial perturbations to observations or dynamics along the rollout induce compounding value-function error and reward-signal collapse; robustness that depends on test-time defenses increases control-loop latency.
- Problem: Evaluating new long-term optimization policies offline is risky because standard OPE can overestimate returns on out-of-distribution state-action pairs, potentially promoting harmful policies to online A/B tests.
- Method:  Propose a Conservative Offline Policy Estimator that performs fitted Q evaluation augmented with a CQL-style regularizer to penalize Q-values for OOD actions, producing a lower-bound estimate of a policy’s per-session long-term value from held-out logs. This estimator guides offline model selection and aligns with observed online outcomes, filtering policies that would degrade user experience.

### Seed b5a4f89f53cb [ROBOTICS] — "Model Learning for Look-ahead Exploration in Continuous Control"
- Retrieved via: Policies fail when small adversarial perturbations to observations or dynamics along the rollout induce compounding value-function error and reward-signal collapse; robustness that depends on test-time defenses increases control-loop latency.
- Problem: Using options as policy building blocks can lead to suboptimal final behavior and sensitivity to the chosen option set; model-based rollouts can suffer from model bias.
- Method:  Use skills only to guide exploration—not to constrain the learned policy—so the final policy remains over primitive actions. Empirically, this look-ahead exploration learns faster and reaches higher success rates than HER, parameterized action space (PAS), hierarchical HER-PAS, and parameter-space noise on Baxter manipulation tasks (pick-and-move, put-in, stack, take-out). The approach remains effective under model mismatch (e.g., an unseen wall) and with reduced skill sets, though learning slows when essential skills like grasp are missing.

### Seed aee1f702cc44 [IR_REC] — "Fast Offline Policy Optimization for Large Scale Recommendation"
- Retrieved via: Retrieval systems fail when small adversarial token or character edits to queries/documents cause large swings in the embedding index and query-document scores, corrupting the candidate pool and lowering recall@k; robustness that relies on serving-time cross-encoder reranking is costly.
- Problem: Serving-time constraints require millisecond top-1 retrieval from large catalogs, and training–serving mismatch or learning full item embeddings can hurt stability and performance.
- Method:  They parameterize the policy with a dot-product form fθ(a,x)=hθ(x)ᵀβa and assume fixed item embeddings β, learning only the user transform hθ(x). A single approximate MIPS index over β is built once and reused both at inference (argmax retrieval) and during training to construct q_{ε,K}, aligning optimization with deployment. Empirically, fixing β has negligible training-time cost and improves performance versus training β (Table 2), supporting the design choice.

### Seed 80c6fcc2f5d6 [IR_REC] — "Multi-View Document Representation Learning for Open-Domain Dense Retrieval"
- Retrieved via: Retrieval systems fail when small adversarial token or character edits to queries/documents cause large swings in the embedding index and query-document scores, corrupting the candidate pool and lowering recall@k; robustness that relies on serving-time cross-encoder reranking is costly.
- Problem: Bi-encoder dense retrievers compress each document into a single vector, which struggles to match documents that can answer multiple, diverse queries; multi-vector alternatives often require dense interactions that break ANN-friendly first-stage retrieval.
- Method:  Introduce multiple [Viewer] tokens into the document input to produce k document embeddings that capture different semantic views, replacing the single [CLS]-based representation. The viewer tokens are randomly initialized and inserted before the document with position ids set to 0 to avoid shifting original token positions. At retrieval time, compute inner-products between the single query embedding and each document viewer embedding, and aggregate via max-pooling; index all viewer embeddings so the approach remains compatible with fast ANN search. This simple architectural change yields multi-view document representations without late-interaction overhead.

### Seed ac9d79a4fd87 [IR_REC] — "Boosting Data Utilization for Multilingual Dense Retrieval"
- Retrieved via: Retrieval systems fail when small adversarial token or character edits to queries/documents cause large swings in the embedding index and query-document scores, corrupting the candidate pool and lowering recall@k; robustness that relies on serving-time cross-encoder reranking is costly.
- Problem: Contrastive fine-tuning of multilingual dense retrievers is hindered by false negatives in hard-negative sets, since top-k retrieved candidates often include relevant but unjudged documents.
- Method:  Build a multilingual hard-negative candidate list by fusing representations from multiple retrievers (e.g., mE5, BGE) via a projection-and-concatenation fusion to score candidates, then use an LLM to judge each candidate’s relevance to the query and its positive (0/1/2 for irrelevant/partially/highly relevant) and keep only those judged irrelevant. This LLM-based filtering eliminates a substantial portion of false negatives (≈20% on average) and yields higher-quality negatives, outperforming strong TopK baselines (e.g., +2.5 nDCG@10 over TopK-PercPos on MIRACL).

### Seed c36ac00d94ea [SPEECH] — "Unlocking Speech Instruction Data Potential with Query Rewriting"
- Retrieved via: ASR systems fail when imperceptible additive perturbations to acoustic frames distort spectrogram features and phoneme alignment, spiking WER; robustness that depends on test-time defenses violates streaming-latency budgets.
- Problem: Validating that TTS-synthesized speech preserves the linguistic content of the source text is unreliable with a single ASR or WER-only checks; ASR errors lead to improper filtering and dataset bias.
- Method:  Use multi-agent annotation and verification: transcribe each synthesized audio with three diverse ASR models (whisper-large-v3, canary-1b, parakeet-tdt-1.1b) and compute semantic similarity to the original text using three embedding models (gte-large-en-v1.5, mxbai-embed-large-v1, stella_en_400M_v5). Average across ASRs and embeddings to form a SIM score and filter with a threshold (α=0.9). This ensemble reduces annotation errors and aligns well with WER (e.g., >98% consistency when WER=0) and improves WER over single-ASR selection (Table 4).

### Seed 38b3a429da83 [SPEECH] — "Multiresolution and Multimodal Speech Recognition with Transformers"
- Retrieved via: ASR systems fail when imperceptible additive perturbations to acoustic frames distort spectrogram features and phoneme alignment, spiking WER; robustness that depends on test-time defenses violates streaming-latency budgets.
- Problem: Audio-only ASR cannot leverage scene context, and traditional AV-ASR (lipreading) requires strict audio–video alignment and camera placement, limiting real-world applicability.
- Method:  Build a Transformer-based AV-ASR with two encoders (audio and video). Project both modalities into a common space via a tied feed-forward layer, then apply cross-modal multi-head scaled dot-product attention where video provides K,V and audio provides Q to map visual context into the audio space. Fuse the attended visual features additively with a learnable scalar α and decode with a Transformer decoder. Trained end-to-end on How2, this fusion yields relative WER gains up to 3.76% over audio-only baselines.

### Seed 1905468c9d3f [SPEECH] — "RED-ACE: Robust Error Detection for ASR using Confidence Embeddings"
- Retrieved via: ASR systems fail when imperceptible additive perturbations to acoustic frames distort spectrogram features and phoneme alignment, spiking WER; robustness that depends on test-time defenses violates streaming-latency budgets.
- Problem: ASR error detection models typically rely only on the transcription text and ignore auxiliary ASR signals like word-level confidences, limiting their ability to accurately flag recognition errors.
- Method:  Introduce RED-ACE, a BERT-based sequence tagger augmented with a confidence embedding layer. ASR word-level confidence scores are quantized into bins and mapped via a learned embedding matrix; the confidence embedding is summed with the token, segment, and position embeddings to form each token’s input. This joint, contextualized encoding of text and confidence yields statistically significant F1 gains over text-only BERT and over naive score fusion (e.g., appending raw scores, BERT C) on LibriSpeech clean/other (e.g., +4.9% and +7.4% F1), remains robust under cross-pool shifts (+6.3% and +9.9% F1), and generalizes across different ASR models (+2.6% and +3.8% F1).

### Seed 8741c91e4338 [ROBOTICS] — "Rearrangement-Based Manipulation via Kinodynamic Planning and Dynamic Planning Horizons"
- Retrieved via: Robotic manipulation fails when slight adversarial disturbances to contact sensing or proprioception destabilize the sensorimotor loop so the manipulation policy deviates from the motion plan; robustness that relies on online replanning with a dynamics model burdens real-time execution.
- Problem: Robotic rearrangement in clutter is hard due to high-dimensional kinodynamics, physical/perception uncertainties, and the impracticality of specifying explicit goal configurations. Open-loop kinodynamic planners often fail on real executions and are inefficient when extensively exploring the full valid state space.
- Method:  Introduce dhRRT, a kinodynamic RRT framework with dynamic planning horizons driven by a task heuristic h(·). The planner monitors progress and, when a heuristic decrease exceeds a threshold p or a maximum tree depth Dmax is reached, executes the best control segment and re-observes the true state, interleaving planning and execution to close the loop. This progress-based execution allows implicit goal specifications via simple heuristics instead of explicit goal states and focuses exploration on task-relevant subspaces. Across grasping, relocating, and sorting, dhRRT improves robustness and efficiency over kdRRT and a replanning baseline (r-kdRRT), handling inaccurate models and nondeterministic physics.

### Seed 1e0dfa0c084a [ROBOTICS] — "Reinforcement Learning on Variable Impedance Controller for High-Precision Robotic Assembly"
- Retrieved via: Robotic manipulation fails when slight adversarial disturbances to contact sensing or proprioception destabilize the sensorimotor loop so the manipulation policy deviates from the motion plan; robustness that relies on online replanning with a dynamics model burdens real-time execution.
- Problem: Tight-tolerance robotic assembly requires precise, contact-aware control, but trajectory-centric RL with direct torque actions struggles to leverage contact constraints and remain stable in free space.
- Method:  Make the policy’s action an operational-space wrench F_tip and execute it with a hybrid motion/force controller: project desired wrenches to joint torques via J^T pseudoinverse, and wrap with a low-gain position loop with motion/force weightings (Σ1, Σ2) for stability. Train a time-varying linear-Gaussian controller with iLQG (with entropy regularization) to output F_tip conditioned on state. This implicitly yields variable impedance—high impedance in free space and high admittance in contact—while exploiting natural task constraints. On four Siemens assembly tasks it substantially outperforms kinematics and torque-control iLQG baselines (e.g., 5/5 vs 0–1/5 successes on Tasks 1–2).

### Seed 67990031a190 [ROBOTICS] — "Impedance Primitive-augmented Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning for Sequential Tasks"
- Retrieved via: Robotic manipulation fails when slight adversarial disturbances to contact sensing or proprioception destabilize the sensorimotor loop so the manipulation policy deviates from the motion plan; robustness that relies on online replanning with a dynamics model burdens real-time execution.
- Problem: Long-horizon, contact-rich manipulation requires adapting interaction stiffness, but existing hierarchical RL frameworks rely on static, position-only primitives, limiting robustness and safety during contact.
- Method:  IMP-HRL augments a hierarchical RL (MAPLE-style) framework with an impedance primitive: the low-level parameter policy outputs both target positions and per-axis stiffness parameters (Kx, Ky, Kz) plus rotational stiffness Kψ, while damping is set via critical damping to reduce learnable parameters. The predicted stiffness serves as an initial estimate for execution, enabling variable-impedance control within sequential primitives. This integration allows the agent to modulate compliance across task phases, improving handling of contact-rich interactions.

You are an ambitious AI researcher scoring NOVELTY of a proposed
method through iterative literature search. Be a HARSH critic — if a
search surfaces published work using the same combination on the same
problem, novelty is low.

## Definition
Novelty = the method introduces a non-trivial combination, mechanism,
or framing that has not been published in this exact form for this
problem. Surface tweaks (renamed hyperparameter, swapped backbone of
equivalent class) and well-established combinations DO NOT qualify
as novel.

## Prior-work signal
Published literature — verified via Semantic Scholar search.
Score the method against literature precedent only; the inspired
seeds shown later are context, NOT a separate scoring axis.

## Score anchors (1-5 integer)

Anchors are written so each level can be supported by what is in the
method text itself plus literature search; do NOT reward "qualitative
trade-off" claims that require running the experiment to verify.

  1 — Identical to a published technique applied to the same problem;
      a search surfaces a paper that matches the method end-to-end.
  2 — Surface variant of a published technique (renamed hyperparameter,
      swapped backbone of an equivalent class, alternate threshold);
      a search surfaces the parent technique on the same problem.
  3 — Combines 2-3 known mechanisms with prior published joint
      application to this problem family. A search surfaces at least
      one paper that pairs the same mechanisms for a closely related
      problem.
  4 — Combines mechanism families with NO surfaced published joint
      application for this problem, OR introduces one named design
      choice (a specific new component or training signal) that is
      not in the surfaced literature. Searches return prior work on
      the individual mechanisms but not their combination as
      proposed.
  5 — Introduces a new mechanism (a named component or training
      signal not present as such in any search result), AND searches
      return no DIRECT near-clone (no paper combining the same
      mechanism families for the same problem in the same
      direction). Related work that pairs ONE of the mechanisms
      with a different counterpart, or uses the same mechanisms for
      a different problem, does NOT block 5; the bar is the absence
      of an end-to-end match.

## Reference examples — 3 query domains

### Block A — "VLM visual token compression"
  Score 5: query-conditional token-merging policy via cross-attention
           scoring tokens in one pass.
  Score 4: static attention pruning + KV-cache eviction policies
           from LLM efficiency — cross-modality non-obvious pairing.
  Score 3: established attention-magnitude pruning applied to ViT
           tokens.
  Score 2: token pruning with a different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of known pruning method.

### Block B — "compositional reasoning with discrete latent structure"
  Score 5: discrete latent composition graphs induced via learned
           merge policy + RL reward shaped by downstream task.
  Score 4: VQ-VAE codes as discrete symbols + neural composition.
  Score 3: ST Gumbel-Softmax for discrete tree induction.
  Score 2: VQ-VAE with renamed quantization function.
  Score 1: standard transformer over text.

### Block C — "time-series anomaly detection in irregular sequences"
  Score 5: time-aware transformer with continuous-time positional
           encoding + downstream-cost-tuned anomaly threshold via
           RL on alarm precision/recall trade-off.
  Score 4: Neural CDE encoder + density-ratio anomaly score —
           combination not surfaced as joint application.
  Score 3: ODE-RNN encoder with reconstruction-error scoring —
           established pattern.
  Score 2: GRU autoencoder on resampled regular intervals with a
           different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of an LSTM forecasting + z-score
           baseline.

## ReAct loop — output format each round (no markdown)

Each round, output EXACTLY ONE of these two formats:

Search format (issue when more evidence is needed):
    Thought: <1-3 sentences on which mechanism pairing you want to
              verify in prior work>
    Query: <semantic scholar search keywords>

Finalize format (issue ONLY when ready to score):
    Reasoning: <2-4 sentences synthesizing what searches found and
                how it maps to a score anchor>
    Prior works: <newline-separated list of papers/methods that most
                  directly cover the proposed method, one per line
                  in the form "paper_title or method_name (year): one-line
                  summary of overlap with the proposed method".
                  Use "(none)" if score is 5 with no near-clone surfaced.>
    Score: <integer 1-5>

Hard rules:
  - DO NOT mix the search and finalize formats. If you output a
    `Query:` line, the caller treats this round as a search and
    ignores any `Score:` you write — do not waste tokens on Score
    in a search round.
  - You MUST issue at least one search before finalizing UNLESS the
    method is a trivial restatement of a known technique.
  - When you write the literal token "Score:" inside Reasoning to
    cite an anchor, write it INLINE (e.g., "matches the Score 4
    anchor"), never on its own line — the parser anchors to lines
    starting with "Score:".
  - Prior works lines must each start with "  - " (two spaces, dash,
    space) so the parser can extract them. Do NOT include a trailing
    "Score:" inside a Prior works line.

## Static context (does not change across rounds)

### Research question
Models are brittle to adversarial perturbations imperceptible to
humans; how can pretraining objectives or data augmentation make
models robust without expensive test-time defenses?


### Proposed method
We propose FACT-MIX pretraining: a dual objective that couples invariance to imperceptible, human-preserving perturbations with explicit sensitivity to semantic, counterfactual edits—so the model learns what to ignore and what to react to. Build two perturbation banks per domain. (a) Factual, perceptual-preserving variants: for vision/audio use small spatial/time shifts, mild blurs, compression, and spectral jitter within perceptual thresholds; for text use paraphrases, synonym/inflection substitutions, punctuation/casing noise, and token/span drops. (b) Counterfactual variants that minimally alter surface form but flip meaning: for text, controlled role swaps or keyword replacements that change predicate–argument structure; for vision/audio, attribute flips or localized edits that change class-defining factors. During pretraining, draw triplets (x, x+, x−) where x+ is factual and x− is counterfactual.

Train a single model with a dual consistency–contrastive loss. First, impose factual consistency: align predictions pθ(y|x) and pθ(y|x+) via symmetric KL and align intermediate representations z(x), z(x+) via cosine or InfoNCE positives. Second, impose counterfactual separation: push apart z(x) and z(x−) with a margin, and apply unlikelihood on labels inconsistent with the counterfactual (penalize high probability of y on x− and of y− on x, x+). To prevent brittleness to token- or patch-level perturbations, add salience-aware masking: randomly drop or synonymize spans/patches while encouraging output stability, and occasionally replace salient spans with semantically opposite content to enforce sensitivity. A lightweight conditioning-dependency regularizer compares the model’s token-level or patch-level probabilities with and without the conditioning signal and penalizes low dependency on the input when predicting content words, discouraging reliance on spurious priors.

Stabilize features against high-frequency, spatially inconsistent noise with gradient-smoothing augmentation. Apply random shifts/jitters and compute a Normalized Discontinuity-style penalty: encourage small changes in feature maps and losses across these micro-transforms. This smoothness term reduces the model’s incentive to encode brittle edges and checkerboard artifacts that adversaries exploit, without introducing test-time defenses. For sequence models, also use selective token mixing: during training, partially replace corrupted or low-confidence tokens with their clean counterparts at a small mixing ratio to prevent cascading error amplification while maintaining exposure to noise.

Finally, equip the encoder with a multi-view aggregation head that learns k latent "view" vectors per example. Feed each perturbed factual view x+ through the shared backbone and aggregate via permutation-invariant pooling (e.g., max or log-sum-exp) over the k latent views to produce a single representation used by the task head. Train with the dual loss on pooled outputs and per-view features; at inference, run a single forward pass with the learned latent views (no input augmentations), so robustness benefits persist without test-time cost.

### Rationale
Adversarial brittleness stems from models exploiting spurious, high-frequency, and position-specific cues and from miscalibration about which input factors truly drive targets. Pretraining with a dual objective addresses both sides: factual consistency teaches invariance to imperceptible, human-preserving perturbations, while counterfactual separation and unlikelihood teach sensitivity when meaning truly changes. This calibrates the decision boundary around human semantics rather than surface quirks.

The gradient-smoothing penalty induced by micro-shifts reduces feature discontinuities that correlate with vulnerability to small Lp perturbations, while selective token mixing prevents instability from compounding errors. The conditioning-dependency regularizer keeps the model anchored to its inputs rather than language or class priors, and the learned multi-view aggregation internalizes robustness to diverse factual perturbations so no ensembles or test-time defenses are needed. All costs are borne at training time; inference uses a single forward pass.

### Inspired seeds (the ideation's input retrieval)
1. [MULTIMODAL] AudioGenX: Explainability on Text-to-Audio Generative Models (2502.00459)
   problem: Text-to-audio generative models lack faithful, token-level explanations of how each word in the prompt influences the generated audio, and attention-based or single-token perturbation methods miss multi-token interactions and are inefficient over long sequences.
   method:  AudioGenX learns a soft importance mask over text tokens for each generated audio token by perturbing the cross-attention mechanism and optimizing an Explainer MLP (sigmoid + Gumbel-softmax) to predict the mask. It jointly uses factual and counterfactual objectives on the latent audio-token embedding e_t: maximize cosine similarity under factual masking and minimize it under counterfactual masking, with L1/L2 regularization for sparse, simple masks. Explanations are computed per audio token (parallelizable) and then aggregated over time to provide holistic or interval-specific explanations. The approach supports simultaneous multi-token perturbations and is model-agnostic for TAG models that use cross-attention.

2. [SPEECH] UniCATS: A Unified Context-Aware Text-to-Speech Framework with Contextual VQ-Diffusion and Vocoding (2306.07547)
   problem: Autoregressive discrete-token TTS models are constrained to left-to-right generation, making them ill-suited for speech editing where both preceding and following contexts must be respected. Using acoustic tokens further adds RVQ prediction complexity and inherits audio quality limits from codec models.
   method:  CTX-txt2vec introduces contextual VQ-diffusion to generate semantic tokens conditioned on both sides of context. It concatenates corrupted target tokens with clean preceding and following context [cA, x_t, cB] and feeds them to a Transformer VQ-diffusion decoder, using a binary indicator embedding to distinguish context vs. target. Text is aligned to token length via a duration predictor and length regulator, and the aligned text encoding is injected by addition to self-attention outputs instead of cross-attention to respect strict alignment. Training randomly samples utterances with both/one/no context, while inference rescales predicted durations to match context speed and iteratively denoises from fully masked tokens.

3. [SPEECH] End-to-end Contrastive Language-Speech Pretraining Model For Long-form Spoken Question Answering (2511.09282)
   problem: Training the non-autoregressive ASR within the retriever is brittle—recognition errors degrade the learned token distributions and downstream retrieval quality; improving the decoder’s context modeling and stability is needed.
   method:  CLSR introduces a sampler for training: after a first-pass decode, it compares predicted tokens with ground truth, and selectively replaces embeddings of misrecognized tokens by their true text embeddings at a mixing ratio λ to form semantic features for a second decoding pass. This stabilizes token distributions used by the VQ adaptor and improves both WER and retrieval recall. Ablations show that removing the VQ quantizer severely harms retrieval, while enabling the sampler improves ASR and retrieval metrics (e.g., on Spoken-SQuAD, WER decreases and R@k increases).

4. [MULTIMODAL] VQS: Linking Segmentations to Questions and Answers for Supervised Attention in VQA and Question-Focused Semantic Segmentation (1708.04686)
   problem: Existing VQA systems lack explicit supervision that links questions/answers to the precise image regions that constitute visual evidence, leading to weak or misaligned attentions; related annotations like human attention maps are coarse and bounding boxes often do not directly answer diverse question types.
   method:  Introduce VQS, a dataset that explicitly links COCO instance segmentations to VQA question–answer pairs by asking annotators to select the segmentation(s) that visually answer each question (with tight boxes when needed). This produces 37,868 images, 96,508 questions, 108,537 segmentations, and 43,725 boxes, enabling transfer of supervision between segmentation and VQA and supporting both supervised attention and new grounded tasks.

5. [MULTIMODAL] ViLT: Vision-and-Language Transformer Without Convolution or Region Supervision (2102.03334)
   problem: Vision-and-Language Pre-training models typically rely on heavy visual embedders (region detectors or deep CNN backbones), making inference slow and constraining expressiveness to a predefined visual vocabulary.
   method:  ViLT replaces region/CNN visual features with a shallow, convolution-free linear patch projection and uses a single-stream transformer to jointly process image patches and text tokens. The transformer is initialized from ViT-B/32 pretrained on ImageNet, with image patches (P=32) and text embeddings concatenated with type embeddings and fed through 12 transformer layers. Pretrained with ITM and MLM, this monolithic design yields large efficiency gains (e.g., ~15 ms vs. ~900 ms for region-feature models; ≥4× faster than grid-feature models) while achieving competitive VQAv2, NLVR2, and retrieval performance.

6. [MULTIMODAL] Multi-Modal Hallucination Control by Visual Information Grounding (2403.14003)
   problem: Autoregressive vision-language models (VLMs) often hallucinate objects or attributes because the model’s reliance on the visual prompt fades as more tokens are generated, and there is no principled, annotation-free way to quantify this grounding loss.
   method:  Introduce a visual Prompt Dependency Measure (PDM) that compares the conditioned distribution p(y_t | y_<t, x, c) to the unconditioned language-only distribution p(y_t | y_<t, x) using distances such as the Hellinger metric (PDM-H). This quantifies how much a token depends on the image versus the language prior. Empirically, PDM decreases with token position (conditioning dilution/fading memory), and lower PDM correlates with increased hallucinated objects in captions, while also identifying ‘contextual pressure’ cases (e.g., function words) where low PDM does not imply hallucination.

7. [LLM_NLP] Tailor: Generating and Perturbing Text with Semantic Controls (2107.07150)
   problem: Controlled text perturbation for evaluation and robustness typically requires task-specific models or sparse controls that don’t specify how to realize targeted edits, making them costly and hard to generalize.
   method:  Tailor finetunes a pretrained seq2seq model (T5) to follow structured control codes derived from PropBank SRL, covering three control types: semantic roles, keyword content with specificity (complete/partial/sparse), and span ordering via predicate form and blank positions. Inputs use a bracketed header of controls plus a context with blanks; outputs explicitly tag generated spans, decoupling semantic intent from surface placement and enabling strict or flexible edits by masking subsets of arguments. To enforce adherence to controls, Tailor adds unlikelihood training with negative samples created by perturbing roles, lemmas, and specificities, yielding higher controllability and closeness than MLE alone.

8. [CV] Overload: Latency Attacks on Object Detection for Edge Devices (2304.05370)
   problem: Existing adversarial attacks on object detectors largely target accuracy, or when targeting latency, rely on complex IoU-based objectives that are computationally and memory intensive—especially problematic on edge devices. The key open question is which model factors most effectively increase inference time with minimal attack cost.
   method:  Overload analyzes NMS time complexity and shows latency is dominated by the number of objects fed into NMS rather than the number of surviving boxes. Based on this, it formulates a simple white-box objective that maximizes per-detection objectness confidence and class probabilities via a monotonic increasing function, inflating the number of pre-NMS objects without tracking pairwise IoUs. The attack is optimized with PGD-style updates under a small perturbation budget and is NMS-agnostic. Empirically, it generates >20k ghost objects and increases end-to-end latency by ~11–13× on Jetson NX, while using less memory/time than Daedalus and Phantom Sponge (e.g., ~3 min for 16 images at 7.5 GB vs. ~145 min for 10 images at 8.5 GB).

9. [RL] Stabilizing Off-Policy Deep Reinforcement Learning from Pixels (2207.00986)
   problem: Off-policy TD-learning from pixel observations is highly unstable: critics quickly overfit to their own noisy targets and fail to learn from sparse, low-magnitude rewards, and it is unclear why image augmentations help.
   method:  The paper diagnoses a 'visual deadly triad'—exclusive reliance on TD-loss, unregularized CNN encoders, and low-magnitude sparse rewards—showing it induces catastrophic self-overfitting. Using offline policy-evaluation on fixed transitions, the authors demonstrate near-perfect correlation to target Q-values but poor correlation to Monte-Carlo returns without augmentations, and that overfitting is tied to high-frequency, spatially inconsistent CNN features. They introduce the Normalized Discontinuity (ND) score to quantify feature/gradient discontinuities, show ND strongly correlates with performance in online and offline training, and explain random shift augmentations as implicit gradient smoothing through shifted computation graphs.

10. [ROBOTICS] Meta Reinforcement Learning for Sim-to-real Domain Adaptation (1909.12906)
   problem: Policies trained in simulation often fail to transfer to real robots under unknown or varying dynamics; domain-randomized policies can adapt unpredictably and even deteriorate during on-policy updates on hardware.
   method:  Use a gradient-based meta-reinforcement learning framework (MAML-like) that trains a meta-policy across tasks defined by randomized dynamics, explicitly optimizing expected return after N inner-loop adaptation steps. At deployment, the policy performs K-rollout inner-loop updates with vanilla policy gradient to quickly adapt to the current dynamics, while the outer meta-update backpropagates through the inner steps and is optimized with PPO. Experiments in simulation and on a KUKA puck-hitting task show stable, consistent adaptation and better performance than a domain-randomization baseline.

11. [IR_REC] Multi-Task Fusion via Reinforcement Learning for Long-Term User Satisfaction in Recommender Systems (2208.04560)
   problem: Evaluating new long-term optimization policies offline is risky because standard OPE can overestimate returns on out-of-distribution state-action pairs, potentially promoting harmful policies to online A/B tests.
   method:  Propose a Conservative Offline Policy Estimator that performs fitted Q evaluation augmented with a CQL-style regularizer to penalize Q-values for OOD actions, producing a lower-bound estimate of a policy’s per-session long-term value from held-out logs. This estimator guides offline model selection and aligns with observed online outcomes, filtering policies that would degrade user experience.

12. [ROBOTICS] Model Learning for Look-ahead Exploration in Continuous Control (1811.08086)
   problem: Using options as policy building blocks can lead to suboptimal final behavior and sensitivity to the chosen option set; model-based rollouts can suffer from model bias.
   method:  Use skills only to guide exploration—not to constrain the learned policy—so the final policy remains over primitive actions. Empirically, this look-ahead exploration learns faster and reaches higher success rates than HER, parameterized action space (PAS), hierarchical HER-PAS, and parameter-space noise on Baxter manipulation tasks (pick-and-move, put-in, stack, take-out). The approach remains effective under model mismatch (e.g., an unseen wall) and with reduced skill sets, though learning slows when essential skills like grasp are missing.

13. [IR_REC] Fast Offline Policy Optimization for Large Scale Recommendation (2208.05327)
   problem: Serving-time constraints require millisecond top-1 retrieval from large catalogs, and training–serving mismatch or learning full item embeddings can hurt stability and performance.
   method:  They parameterize the policy with a dot-product form fθ(a,x)=hθ(x)ᵀβa and assume fixed item embeddings β, learning only the user transform hθ(x). A single approximate MIPS index over β is built once and reused both at inference (argmax retrieval) and during training to construct q_{ε,K}, aligning optimization with deployment. Empirically, fixing β has negligible training-time cost and improves performance versus training β (Table 2), supporting the design choice.

14. [IR_REC] Multi-View Document Representation Learning for Open-Domain Dense Retrieval (2203.08372)
   problem: Bi-encoder dense retrievers compress each document into a single vector, which struggles to match documents that can answer multiple, diverse queries; multi-vector alternatives often require dense interactions that break ANN-friendly first-stage retrieval.
   method:  Introduce multiple [Viewer] tokens into the document input to produce k document embeddings that capture different semantic views, replacing the single [CLS]-based representation. The viewer tokens are randomly initialized and inserted before the document with position ids set to 0 to avoid shifting original token positions. At retrieval time, compute inner-products between the single query embedding and each document viewer embedding, and aggregate via max-pooling; index all viewer embeddings so the approach remains compatible with fast ANN search. This simple architectural change yields multi-view document representations without late-interaction overhead.

15. [IR_REC] Boosting Data Utilization for Multilingual Dense Retrieval (2509.09459)
   problem: Contrastive fine-tuning of multilingual dense retrievers is hindered by false negatives in hard-negative sets, since top-k retrieved candidates often include relevant but unjudged documents.
   method:  Build a multilingual hard-negative candidate list by fusing representations from multiple retrievers (e.g., mE5, BGE) via a projection-and-concatenation fusion to score candidates, then use an LLM to judge each candidate’s relevance to the query and its positive (0/1/2 for irrelevant/partially/highly relevant) and keep only those judged irrelevant. This LLM-based filtering eliminates a substantial portion of false negatives (≈20% on average) and yields higher-quality negatives, outperforming strong TopK baselines (e.g., +2.5 nDCG@10 over TopK-PercPos on MIRACL).

16. [SPEECH] Unlocking Speech Instruction Data Potential with Query Rewriting (2507.08603)
   problem: Validating that TTS-synthesized speech preserves the linguistic content of the source text is unreliable with a single ASR or WER-only checks; ASR errors lead to improper filtering and dataset bias.
   method:  Use multi-agent annotation and verification: transcribe each synthesized audio with three diverse ASR models (whisper-large-v3, canary-1b, parakeet-tdt-1.1b) and compute semantic similarity to the original text using three embedding models (gte-large-en-v1.5, mxbai-embed-large-v1, stella_en_400M_v5). Average across ASRs and embeddings to form a SIM score and filter with a threshold (α=0.9). This ensemble reduces annotation errors and aligns well with WER (e.g., >98% consistency when WER=0) and improves WER over single-ASR selection (Table 4).

17. [SPEECH] Multiresolution and Multimodal Speech Recognition with Transformers (2004.14840)
   problem: Audio-only ASR cannot leverage scene context, and traditional AV-ASR (lipreading) requires strict audio–video alignment and camera placement, limiting real-world applicability.
   method:  Build a Transformer-based AV-ASR with two encoders (audio and video). Project both modalities into a common space via a tied feed-forward layer, then apply cross-modal multi-head scaled dot-product attention where video provides K,V and audio provides Q to map visual context into the audio space. Fuse the attended visual features additively with a learnable scalar α and decode with a Transformer decoder. Trained end-to-end on How2, this fusion yields relative WER gains up to 3.76% over audio-only baselines.

18. [SPEECH] RED-ACE: Robust Error Detection for ASR using Confidence Embeddings (2203.07172)
   problem: ASR error detection models typically rely only on the transcription text and ignore auxiliary ASR signals like word-level confidences, limiting their ability to accurately flag recognition errors.
   method:  Introduce RED-ACE, a BERT-based sequence tagger augmented with a confidence embedding layer. ASR word-level confidence scores are quantized into bins and mapped via a learned embedding matrix; the confidence embedding is summed with the token, segment, and position embeddings to form each token’s input. This joint, contextualized encoding of text and confidence yields statistically significant F1 gains over text-only BERT and over naive score fusion (e.g., appending raw scores, BERT C) on LibriSpeech clean/other (e.g., +4.9% and +7.4% F1), remains robust under cross-pool shifts (+6.3% and +9.9% F1), and generalizes across different ASR models (+2.6% and +3.8% F1).

19. [ROBOTICS] Rearrangement-Based Manipulation via Kinodynamic Planning and Dynamic Planning Horizons (2208.02312)
   problem: Robotic rearrangement in clutter is hard due to high-dimensional kinodynamics, physical/perception uncertainties, and the impracticality of specifying explicit goal configurations. Open-loop kinodynamic planners often fail on real executions and are inefficient when extensively exploring the full valid state space.
   method:  Introduce dhRRT, a kinodynamic RRT framework with dynamic planning horizons driven by a task heuristic h(·). The planner monitors progress and, when a heuristic decrease exceeds a threshold p or a maximum tree depth Dmax is reached, executes the best control segment and re-observes the true state, interleaving planning and execution to close the loop. This progress-based execution allows implicit goal specifications via simple heuristics instead of explicit goal states and focuses exploration on task-relevant subspaces. Across grasping, relocating, and sorting, dhRRT improves robustness and efficiency over kdRRT and a replanning baseline (r-kdRRT), handling inaccurate models and nondeterministic physics.

20. [ROBOTICS] Reinforcement Learning on Variable Impedance Controller for High-Precision Robotic Assembly (1903.01066)
   problem: Tight-tolerance robotic assembly requires precise, contact-aware control, but trajectory-centric RL with direct torque actions struggles to leverage contact constraints and remain stable in free space.
   method:  Make the policy’s action an operational-space wrench F_tip and execute it with a hybrid motion/force controller: project desired wrenches to joint torques via J^T pseudoinverse, and wrap with a low-gain position loop with motion/force weightings (Σ1, Σ2) for stability. Train a time-varying linear-Gaussian controller with iLQG (with entropy regularization) to output F_tip conditioned on state. This implicitly yields variable impedance—high impedance in free space and high admittance in contact—while exploiting natural task constraints. On four Siemens assembly tasks it substantially outperforms kinematics and torque-control iLQG baselines (e.g., 5/5 vs 0–1/5 successes on Tasks 1–2).

21. [ROBOTICS] Impedance Primitive-augmented Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning for Sequential Tasks (2508.19607)
   problem: Long-horizon, contact-rich manipulation requires adapting interaction stiffness, but existing hierarchical RL frameworks rely on static, position-only primitives, limiting robustness and safety during contact.
   method:  IMP-HRL augments a hierarchical RL (MAPLE-style) framework with an impedance primitive: the low-level parameter policy outputs both target positions and per-axis stiffness parameters (Kx, Ky, Kz) plus rotational stiffness Kψ, while damping is set via critical damping to reduce learnable parameters. The predicted stiffness serves as an initial estimate for execution, enabling variable-impedance control within sequential primitives. This integration allows the agent to modulate compliance across task phases, improving handling of contact-rich interactions.

## Tie-breaking
When between two adjacent scores, default to the LOWER. Resist
optimism bias toward "novel". Score against the literature
signal only; do not apply an additional penalty when the method
reuses inspired seeds (the seeds are context, not a benchmark).

Round 1/10.

Last search results:
(empty on first round — issue your first search)

Either issue a Search (Thought + Query) or Finalize (Reasoning + Prior works + Score).

messages.content:
You are an ambitious AI researcher scoring NOVELTY of a proposed
method through iterative literature search. Be a HARSH critic — if a
search surfaces published work using the same combination on the same
problem, novelty is low.

## Definition
Novelty = the method introduces a non-trivial combination, mechanism,
or framing that has not been published in this exact form for this
problem. Surface tweaks (renamed hyperparameter, swapped backbone of
equivalent class) and well-established combinations DO NOT qualify
as novel.

## Prior-work signal
Published literature — verified via Semantic Scholar search.
Score the method against literature precedent only; the inspired
seeds shown later are context, NOT a separate scoring axis.

## Score anchors (1-5 integer)

Anchors are written so each level can be supported by what is in the
method text itself plus literature search; do NOT reward "qualitative
trade-off" claims that require running the experiment to verify.

  1 — Identical to a published technique applied to the same problem;
      a search surfaces a paper that matches the method end-to-end.
  2 — Surface variant of a published technique (renamed hyperparameter,
      swapped backbone of an equivalent class, alternate threshold);
      a search surfaces the parent technique on the same problem.
  3 — Combines 2-3 known mechanisms with prior published joint
      application to this problem family. A search surfaces at least
      one paper that pairs the same mechanisms for a closely related
      problem.
  4 — Combines mechanism families with NO surfaced published joint
      application for this problem, OR introduces one named design
      choice (a specific new component or training signal) that is
      not in the surfaced literature. Searches return prior work on
      the individual mechanisms but not their combination as
      proposed.
  5 — Introduces a new mechanism (a named component or training
      signal not present as such in any search result), AND searches
      return no DIRECT near-clone (no paper combining the same
      mechanism families for the same problem in the same
      direction). Related work that pairs ONE of the mechanisms
      with a different counterpart, or uses the same mechanisms for
      a different problem, does NOT block 5; the bar is the absence
      of an end-to-end match.

## Reference examples — 3 query domains

### Block A — "VLM visual token compression"
  Score 5: query-conditional token-merging policy via cross-attention
           scoring tokens in one pass.
  Score 4: static attention pruning + KV-cache eviction policies
           from LLM efficiency — cross-modality non-obvious pairing.
  Score 3: established attention-magnitude pruning applied to ViT
           tokens.
  Score 2: token pruning with a different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of known pruning method.

### Block B — "compositional reasoning with discrete latent structure"
  Score 5: discrete latent composition graphs induced via learned
           merge policy + RL reward shaped by downstream task.
  Score 4: VQ-VAE codes as discrete symbols + neural composition.
  Score 3: ST Gumbel-Softmax for discrete tree induction.
  Score 2: VQ-VAE with renamed quantization function.
  Score 1: standard transformer over text.

### Block C — "time-series anomaly detection in irregular sequences"
  Score 5: time-aware transformer with continuous-time positional
           encoding + downstream-cost-tuned anomaly threshold via
           RL on alarm precision/recall trade-off.
  Score 4: Neural CDE encoder + density-ratio anomaly score —
           combination not surfaced as joint application.
  Score 3: ODE-RNN encoder with reconstruction-error scoring —
           established pattern.
  Score 2: GRU autoencoder on resampled regular intervals with a
           different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of an LSTM forecasting + z-score
           baseline.

## ReAct loop — output format each round (no markdown)

Each round, output EXACTLY ONE of these two formats:

Search format (issue when more evidence is needed):
    Thought: <1-3 sentences on which mechanism pairing you want to
              verify in prior work>
    Query: <semantic scholar search keywords>

Finalize format (issue ONLY when ready to score):
    Reasoning: <2-4 sentences synthesizing what searches found and
                how it maps to a score anchor>
    Prior works: <newline-separated list of papers/methods that most
                  directly cover the proposed method, one per line
                  in the form "paper_title or method_name (year): one-line
                  summary of overlap with the proposed method".
                  Use "(none)" if score is 5 with no near-clone surfaced.>
    Score: <integer 1-5>
