1. When Does Contrastive Learning Preserve Adversarial Robustness from Pretraining to Finetuning? (Neural Information Processing Systems 2021, cites: 141)
   Contrastive learning (CL) can learn generalizable feature representations and achieve the state-of-the-art performance of downstream tasks by finetuning a linear classifier on top of it. However, as adversarial robustness becomes vital in image classification, it remains unclear whether or not CL is able to preserve robustness to downstream tasks. The main challenge is that in the self-supervised pretraining + supervised finetuning paradigm, adversarial robustness is easily forgotten due to a learning task mismatch from pretraining to finetuning. We call such a challenge 'cross-task robustness transferability'. To address the above problem, in this paper we revisit and advance CL principles through the lens of robustness enhancement. We show that (1) the design of contrastive views matters: High-frequency components of images are beneficial to improving model robustness; (2) Augmenting CL with pseudo-supervision stimulus (e.g., resorting to feature clustering) helps preserve robustness without forgetting. Equipped with our new designs, we propose AdvCL, a novel adversarial contrastive pretraining framework. We show that AdvCL is able to enhance cross-task robustness transferability without loss of model accuracy and finetuning efficiency. With a thorough experimental study, we demonstrate that AdvCL outperforms the state-of-the-art self-supervised robust learning methods across multiple datasets (CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and STL-10) and finetuning schemes (linear evaluation and full model finetuning).

2. Counterfactual contrastive learning: robust representations via causal image synthesis (DEMI@MICCAI 2024, cites: 12)
   Contrastive pretraining is well-known to improve downstream task performance and model generalisation, especially in limited label settings. However, it is sensitive to the choice of augmentation pipeline. Positive pairs should preserve semantic information while destroying domain-specific information. Standard augmentation pipelines emulate domain-specific changes with pre-defined photometric transformations, but what if we could simulate realistic domain changes instead? In this work, we show how to utilise recent progress in counterfactual image generation to this effect. We propose CF-SimCLR, a counterfactual contrastive learning approach which leverages approximate counterfactual inference for positive pair creation. Comprehensive evaluation across five datasets, on chest radiography and mammography, demonstrates that CF-SimCLR substantially improves robustness to acquisition shift with higher downstream performance on both in- and out-of-distribution data, particularly for domains which are under-represented during training.

3. Exploring the Adversarial Robustness of CLIP for AI-generated Image Detection (International Workshop on Information Forensics and Security 2024, cites: 19)
   In recent years, many forensic detectors have been proposed to detect AI-generated images and prevent their use for malicious purposes. Convolutional neural networks (CNNs) have long been the dominant architecture in this field and have been the subject of intense study. However, recently proposed Transformer-based detectors have been shown to match or even outperform CNN-based detectors, especially in terms of generalization. In this paper, we study the adversarial robustness of AI-generated image detectors, focusing on Contrastive LanguageImage Pretraining (CLIP)-based methods that rely on Visual Transformer (ViT) backbones and comparing their performance with CNN-based methods. We study the robustness to different adversarial attacks under a variety of conditions and analyze both numerical results and frequency-domain patterns. CLIPbased detectors are found to be vulnerable to white-box attacks justlike CNN-based detectors. However, attacks do not easily transfer between CNN-based and CLIP-based methods. This is also confirmed by the different distribution of the adversarial noise patterns in the frequency domain. Overall, this analysis provides new insights into the properties of forensic detectors that can help to develop more effective strategies.

4. RoCBert: Robust Chinese Bert with Multimodal Contrastive Pretraining (Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics 2022, cites: 38)
   Large-scale pretrained language models have achieved SOTA results on NLP tasks. However, they have been shown vulnerable to adversarial attacks especially for logographic languages like Chinese. In this work, we propose RoCBert: a pretrained Chinese Bert that is robust to various forms of adversarial attacks like word perturbation, synonyms, typos, etc. It is pretrained with the contrastive learning objective which maximizes the label consistency under different synthesized adversarial examples. The model takes as input multimodal information including the semantic, phonetic and visual features. We show all these features areimportant to the model robustness since the attack can be performed in all the three forms. Across 5 Chinese NLU tasks, RoCBert outperforms strong baselines under three blackbox adversarial algorithms without sacrificing the performance on clean testset. It also performs the best in the toxic content detection task under human-made attacks.

5. Robust image representations with counterfactual contrastive learning (Medical Image Anal. 2024, cites: 14)
   Contrastive pretraining can substantially increase model generalisation and downstream performance. However, the quality of the learned representations is highly dependent on the data augmentation strategy applied to generate positive pairs. Positive contrastive pairs should preserve semantic meaning while discarding unwanted variations related to the data acquisition domain. Traditional contrastive pipelines attempt to simulate domain shifts through pre-defined generic image transformations. However, these do not always mimic realistic and relevant domain variations for medical imaging, such as scanner differences. To tackle this issue, we herein introduce counterfactual contrastive learning, a novel framework leveraging recent advances in causal image synthesis to create contrastive positive pairs that faithfully capture relevant domain variations. Our method, evaluated across five datasets encompassing both chest radiography and mammography data, for two established contrastive objectives (SimCLR and DINO-v2), outperforms standard contrastive learning in terms of robustness to acquisition shift. Notably, counterfactual contrastive learning achieves superior downstream performance on both in-distribution and external datasets, especially for images acquired with scanners under-represented in the training set. Further experiments show that the proposed framework extends beyond acquisition shifts, with models trained with counterfactual contrastive learning reducing subgroup disparities across biological sex.

6. A Constraint-Aware Framework for Adversarial Robustness in UPI Fraud Detection (2025 International Conference on Sustainable Communication Networks and Application (ICSCN) 2025, cites: 0)
   The rise of real-time payment systems such as India’s Unified Payments Interface (UPI) has transformed transaction behavior while introducing new security challenges. Al-though machine learning is widely used for fraud detection, most prior work studies adversarial robustness in experimental settings that do not reflect operational constraints of real payment systems. We propose a framework that incorporates UPI-specific limitations — fixed transaction attributes and rate caps — into adversarial robustness evaluation. Under realistic, constraint-aware attacks, we show that standard models (e.g., XGBoost) can be severely degraded (F1 falling from 0.71 to 0.04). SHAP-based analysis reveals how adversarial manipulations distort model reasoning. To defend against such attacks, we introduce UPI-Counterfactual Invariance Regularization (UPI-CIR), a training regularizer that enforces prediction invariance to constrained perturbations. Compared with conventional adversarial training, UPI-CIR substantially improves survivability under attack (F1 = 0.82) and also yields better performance on clean data. Our work establishes a more realistic standard for adversarial testing in financial systems and proposes a practical, domain-aware defense for securing modern payment infrastructures.

7. ArmorCLIP: a hybrid defense strategy for boosting adversarial robustness in vision-language models (Other Conferences 2024, cites: 1)
   The robustness of Vision-Language Models (VLMs) such as Contrastive Language-Image Pretraining (CLIP) is critical for their deployment in safety-critical applications like autonomous driving, healthcare diagnostics, and security systems, where accurate interpretation of visual and textual data is essential. However, these models are highly susceptible to adversarial attacks, which can severely compromise their performance and reliability in real-world scenarios. Previous methods have primarily focused on improving robustness through adversarial training and generating adversarial examples using models like FGSM, AutoAttack, and DeepFool. However, these approaches often rely on strong assumptions, such as fixed perturbation norms or predefined attack patterns, and involve high computational complexity, making them challenging to implement in practical settings. In this paper, we propose a novel adversarial training framework that integrates multiple attack strategies and advanced machine learning techniques to significantly enhance the robustness of VLMs against a broad range of adversarial attacks. Experiments conducted on real-world datasets, including CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100, demonstrate that the proposed method significantly enhances model robustness. The fine-tuned CLIP model achieved an accuracy of 43.5% on adversarially perturbed images, compared to only 4% for the baseline model. The neural network model achieved a high accuracy of 98% in these challenging classification tasks, while the XGBoost model reached a success rate of 85.26% in prediction tasks.

8. Finetune Like You Pretrain: Boosting Zero-shot Adversarial Robustness in Vision-language Models ( 2026, cites: 1)
   Despite their impressive zero-shot abilities, vision-language models such as CLIP have been shown to be susceptible to adversarial attacks. To enhance its adversarial robustness, recent studies finetune the pretrained vision encoder of CLIP with adversarial examples on a proxy dataset such as ImageNet by aligning adversarial images with correct class labels. However, these methods overlook the important roles of training data distributions and learning objectives, resulting in reduced zero-shot capabilities and limited transferability of robustness across domains and datasets. In this work, we propose a simple yet effective paradigm AdvFLYP, which follows the training recipe of CLIP's pretraining process when performing adversarial finetuning to the model. Specifically, AdvFLYP finetunes CLIP with adversarial images created based on image-text pairs collected from the web, and match them with their corresponding texts via a contrastive loss. To alleviate distortion of adversarial image embeddings of noisy web images, we further propose to regularise AdvFLYP by penalising deviation of adversarial image features. We show that logit- and feature-level regularisation terms benefit robustness and clean accuracy, respectively. Extensive experiments on 14 downstream datasets spanning various domains show the superiority of our paradigm over mainstream practices. Our code and model weights are released at https://github.com/Sxing2/AdvFLYP.

9. Multimodal Pain Recognition Based on Contrastive Adversarial Autoencoder Pretraining (Machine Learning and Knowledge Extraction 2025, cites: 0)
   Background: Automated pain assessment aims to enable objective measurement of patients’ individual pain experiences for improving health care and conserving medical staff. This is particularly important for patients with a disability to communicate caused by mental impairments, unconsciousness, or infantile restrictions. When operating in the critical domain of health care, where wrong decisions harbor the risk of reducing patients’ quality of life—or even result in life-threatening conditions—multimodal pain assessment systems are the preferred choice to facilitate robust decision-making and to maximize resilience against partial sensor outages. Methods: Hence, we propose the MultiModal Supervised Contrastive Adversarial AutoEncoder (MM-SCAAE) pretraining framework for multi-sensor information fusion. Specifically, we implement an application-specific model to accomplish the task of pain recognition using biopotentials from the publicly available heat pain database BioVid. Results: Our model reaches new state-of-the-art performance for multimodal classification regarding all pain recognition tasks of ‘no pain’ versus ‘pain intensity’. For the most relevant task of ‘no pain’ versus ‘highest pain’, we achieve 84.22% accuracy (F1-score: 83.72%), which can be boosted in practice to an accuracy of ≈95% through grouped-prediction estimates. Conclusions: The generic MM-SCAAE framework offers promising perspectives for multimodal representation learning.

10. Revisiting the Adversarial Robustness of Vision Language Models: a Multimodal Perspective (arXiv.org 2024, cites: 28)
   Pretrained vision-language models (VLMs) like CLIP exhibit exceptional generalization across diverse downstream tasks. While recent studies reveal their vulnerability to adversarial attacks, research to date has primarily focused on enhancing the robustness of image encoders against image-based attacks, with defenses against text-based and multimodal attacks remaining largely unexplored. To this end, this work presents the first comprehensive study on improving the adversarial robustness of VLMs against attacks targeting image, text, and multimodal inputs. This is achieved by proposing multimodal contrastive adversarial training (MMCoA). Such an approach strengthens the robustness of both image and text encoders by aligning the clean text embeddings with adversarial image embeddings, and adversarial text embeddings with clean image embeddings. The robustness of the proposed MMCoA is examined against existing defense methods over image, text, and multimodal attacks on the CLIP model. Extensive experiments on 15 datasets across two tasks reveal the characteristics of different adversarial defense methods under distinct distribution shifts and dataset complexities across the three attack types. This paves the way for a unified framework of adversarial robustness against different modality attacks, opening up new possibilities for securing VLMs against multimodal attacks. The code is available at https://github.com/ElleZWQ/MMCoA.git.

messages.content:
You are an ambitious AI researcher scoring NOVELTY of a proposed
method through iterative literature search. Be a HARSH critic — if a
search surfaces published work using the same combination on the same
problem, novelty is low.

## Definition
Novelty = the method introduces a non-trivial combination, mechanism,
or framing that has not been published in this exact form for this
problem. Surface tweaks (renamed hyperparameter, swapped backbone of
equivalent class) and well-established combinations DO NOT qualify
as novel.

## Prior-work signal
Published literature — verified via Semantic Scholar search.
Score the method against literature precedent only; the inspired
seeds shown later are context, NOT a separate scoring axis.

## Score anchors (1-5 integer)

Anchors are written so each level can be supported by what is in the
method text itself plus literature search; do NOT reward "qualitative
trade-off" claims that require running the experiment to verify.

  1 — Identical to a published technique applied to the same problem;
      a search surfaces a paper that matches the method end-to-end.
  2 — Surface variant of a published technique (renamed hyperparameter,
      swapped backbone of an equivalent class, alternate threshold);
      a search surfaces the parent technique on the same problem.
  3 — Combines 2-3 known mechanisms with prior published joint
      application to this problem family. A search surfaces at least
      one paper that pairs the same mechanisms for a closely related
      problem.
  4 — Combines mechanism families with NO surfaced published joint
      application for this problem, OR introduces one named design
      choice (a specific new component or training signal) that is
      not in the surfaced literature. Searches return prior work on
      the individual mechanisms but not their combination as
      proposed.
  5 — Introduces a new mechanism (a named component or training
      signal not present as such in any search result), AND searches
      return no DIRECT near-clone (no paper combining the same
      mechanism families for the same problem in the same
      direction). Related work that pairs ONE of the mechanisms
      with a different counterpart, or uses the same mechanisms for
      a different problem, does NOT block 5; the bar is the absence
      of an end-to-end match.

## Reference examples — 3 query domains

### Block A — "VLM visual token compression"
  Score 5: query-conditional token-merging policy via cross-attention
           scoring tokens in one pass.
  Score 4: static attention pruning + KV-cache eviction policies
           from LLM efficiency — cross-modality non-obvious pairing.
  Score 3: established attention-magnitude pruning applied to ViT
           tokens.
  Score 2: token pruning with a different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of known pruning method.

### Block B — "compositional reasoning with discrete latent structure"
  Score 5: discrete latent composition graphs induced via learned
           merge policy + RL reward shaped by downstream task.
  Score 4: VQ-VAE codes as discrete symbols + neural composition.
  Score 3: ST Gumbel-Softmax for discrete tree induction.
  Score 2: VQ-VAE with renamed quantization function.
  Score 1: standard transformer over text.

### Block C — "time-series anomaly detection in irregular sequences"
  Score 5: time-aware transformer with continuous-time positional
           encoding + downstream-cost-tuned anomaly threshold via
           RL on alarm precision/recall trade-off.
  Score 4: Neural CDE encoder + density-ratio anomaly score —
           combination not surfaced as joint application.
  Score 3: ODE-RNN encoder with reconstruction-error scoring —
           established pattern.
  Score 2: GRU autoencoder on resampled regular intervals with a
           different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of an LSTM forecasting + z-score
           baseline.

## ReAct loop — output format each round (no markdown)

Each round, output EXACTLY ONE of these two formats:

Search format (issue when more evidence is needed):
    Thought: <1-3 sentences on which mechanism pairing you want to
              verify in prior work>
    Query: <semantic scholar search keywords>

Finalize format (issue ONLY when ready to score):
    Reasoning: <2-4 sentences synthesizing what searches found and
                how it maps to a score anchor>
    Prior works: <newline-separated list of papers/methods that most
                  directly cover the proposed method, one per line
                  in the form "paper_title or method_name (year): one-line
                  summary of overlap with the proposed method".
                  Use "(none)" if score is 5 with no near-clone surfaced.>
    Score: <integer 1-5>

Hard rules:
  - DO NOT mix the search and finalize formats. If you output a
    `Query:` line, the caller treats this round as a search and
    ignores any `Score:` you write — do not waste tokens on Score
    in a search round.
  - You MUST issue at least one search before finalizing UNLESS the
    method is a trivial restatement of a known technique.
  - When you write the literal token "Score:" inside Reasoning to
    cite an anchor, write it INLINE (e.g., "matches the Score 4
    anchor"), never on its own line — the parser anchors to lines
    starting with "Score:".
  - Prior works lines must each start with "  - " (two spaces, dash,
    space) so the parser can extract them. Do NOT include a trailing
    "Score:" inside a Prior works line.

## Static context (does not change across rounds)

### Research question
Models are brittle to adversarial perturbations imperceptible to
humans; how can pretraining objectives or data augmentation make
models robust without expensive test-time defenses?


### Proposed method
We propose FACT-MIX pretraining: a dual objective that couples invariance to imperceptible, human-preserving perturbations with explicit sensitivity to semantic, counterfactual edits—so the model learns what to ignore and what to react to. Build two perturbation banks per domain. (a) Factual, perceptual-preserving variants: for vision/audio use small spatial/time shifts, mild blurs, compression, and spectral jitter within perceptual thresholds; for text use paraphrases, synonym/inflection substitutions, punctuation/casing noise, and token/span drops. (b) Counterfactual variants that minimally alter surface form but flip meaning: for text, controlled role swaps or keyword replacements that change predicate–argument structure; for vision/audio, attribute flips or localized edits that change class-defining factors. During pretraining, draw triplets (x, x+, x−) where x+ is factual and x− is counterfactual.

Train a single model with a dual consistency–contrastive loss. First, impose factual consistency: align predictions pθ(y|x) and pθ(y|x+) via symmetric KL and align intermediate representations z(x), z(x+) via cosine or InfoNCE positives. Second, impose counterfactual separation: push apart z(x) and z(x−) with a margin, and apply unlikelihood on labels inconsistent with the counterfactual (penalize high probability of y on x− and of y− on x, x+). To prevent brittleness to token- or patch-level perturbations, add salience-aware masking: randomly drop or synonymize spans/patches while encouraging output stability, and occasionally replace salient spans with semantically opposite content to enforce sensitivity. A lightweight conditioning-dependency regularizer compares the model’s token-level or patch-level probabilities with and without the conditioning signal and penalizes low dependency on the input when predicting content words, discouraging reliance on spurious priors.

Stabilize features against high-frequency, spatially inconsistent noise with gradient-smoothing augmentation. Apply random shifts/jitters and compute a Normalized Discontinuity-style penalty: encourage small changes in feature maps and losses across these micro-transforms. This smoothness term reduces the model’s incentive to encode brittle edges and checkerboard artifacts that adversaries exploit, without introducing test-time defenses. For sequence models, also use selective token mixing: during training, partially replace corrupted or low-confidence tokens with their clean counterparts at a small mixing ratio to prevent cascading error amplification while maintaining exposure to noise.

Finally, equip the encoder with a multi-view aggregation head that learns k latent "view" vectors per example. Feed each perturbed factual view x+ through the shared backbone and aggregate via permutation-invariant pooling (e.g., max or log-sum-exp) over the k latent views to produce a single representation used by the task head. Train with the dual loss on pooled outputs and per-view features; at inference, run a single forward pass with the learned latent views (no input augmentations), so robustness benefits persist without test-time cost.

### Rationale
Adversarial brittleness stems from models exploiting spurious, high-frequency, and position-specific cues and from miscalibration about which input factors truly drive targets. Pretraining with a dual objective addresses both sides: factual consistency teaches invariance to imperceptible, human-preserving perturbations, while counterfactual separation and unlikelihood teach sensitivity when meaning truly changes. This calibrates the decision boundary around human semantics rather than surface quirks.

The gradient-smoothing penalty induced by micro-shifts reduces feature discontinuities that correlate with vulnerability to small Lp perturbations, while selective token mixing prevents instability from compounding errors. The conditioning-dependency regularizer keeps the model anchored to its inputs rather than language or class priors, and the learned multi-view aggregation internalizes robustness to diverse factual perturbations so no ensembles or test-time defenses are needed. All costs are borne at training time; inference uses a single forward pass.

### Inspired seeds (the ideation's input retrieval)
1. [MULTIMODAL] AudioGenX: Explainability on Text-to-Audio Generative Models (2502.00459)
   problem: Text-to-audio generative models lack faithful, token-level explanations of how each word in the prompt influences the generated audio, and attention-based or single-token perturbation methods miss multi-token interactions and are inefficient over long sequences.
   method:  AudioGenX learns a soft importance mask over text tokens for each generated audio token by perturbing the cross-attention mechanism and optimizing an Explainer MLP (sigmoid + Gumbel-softmax) to predict the mask. It jointly uses factual and counterfactual objectives on the latent audio-token embedding e_t: maximize cosine similarity under factual masking and minimize it under counterfactual masking, with L1/L2 regularization for sparse, simple masks. Explanations are computed per audio token (parallelizable) and then aggregated over time to provide holistic or interval-specific explanations. The approach supports simultaneous multi-token perturbations and is model-agnostic for TAG models that use cross-attention.

2. [SPEECH] UniCATS: A Unified Context-Aware Text-to-Speech Framework with Contextual VQ-Diffusion and Vocoding (2306.07547)
   problem: Autoregressive discrete-token TTS models are constrained to left-to-right generation, making them ill-suited for speech editing where both preceding and following contexts must be respected. Using acoustic tokens further adds RVQ prediction complexity and inherits audio quality limits from codec models.
   method:  CTX-txt2vec introduces contextual VQ-diffusion to generate semantic tokens conditioned on both sides of context. It concatenates corrupted target tokens with clean preceding and following context [cA, x_t, cB] and feeds them to a Transformer VQ-diffusion decoder, using a binary indicator embedding to distinguish context vs. target. Text is aligned to token length via a duration predictor and length regulator, and the aligned text encoding is injected by addition to self-attention outputs instead of cross-attention to respect strict alignment. Training randomly samples utterances with both/one/no context, while inference rescales predicted durations to match context speed and iteratively denoises from fully masked tokens.

3. [SPEECH] End-to-end Contrastive Language-Speech Pretraining Model For Long-form Spoken Question Answering (2511.09282)
   problem: Training the non-autoregressive ASR within the retriever is brittle—recognition errors degrade the learned token distributions and downstream retrieval quality; improving the decoder’s context modeling and stability is needed.
   method:  CLSR introduces a sampler for training: after a first-pass decode, it compares predicted tokens with ground truth, and selectively replaces embeddings of misrecognized tokens by their true text embeddings at a mixing ratio λ to form semantic features for a second decoding pass. This stabilizes token distributions used by the VQ adaptor and improves both WER and retrieval recall. Ablations show that removing the VQ quantizer severely harms retrieval, while enabling the sampler improves ASR and retrieval metrics (e.g., on Spoken-SQuAD, WER decreases and R@k increases).

4. [MULTIMODAL] VQS: Linking Segmentations to Questions and Answers for Supervised Attention in VQA and Question-Focused Semantic Segmentation (1708.04686)
   problem: Existing VQA systems lack explicit supervision that links questions/answers to the precise image regions that constitute visual evidence, leading to weak or misaligned attentions; related annotations like human attention maps are coarse and bounding boxes often do not directly answer diverse question types.
   method:  Introduce VQS, a dataset that explicitly links COCO instance segmentations to VQA question–answer pairs by asking annotators to select the segmentation(s) that visually answer each question (with tight boxes when needed). This produces 37,868 images, 96,508 questions, 108,537 segmentations, and 43,725 boxes, enabling transfer of supervision between segmentation and VQA and supporting both supervised attention and new grounded tasks.

5. [MULTIMODAL] ViLT: Vision-and-Language Transformer Without Convolution or Region Supervision (2102.03334)
   problem: Vision-and-Language Pre-training models typically rely on heavy visual embedders (region detectors or deep CNN backbones), making inference slow and constraining expressiveness to a predefined visual vocabulary.
   method:  ViLT replaces region/CNN visual features with a shallow, convolution-free linear patch projection and uses a single-stream transformer to jointly process image patches and text tokens. The transformer is initialized from ViT-B/32 pretrained on ImageNet, with image patches (P=32) and text embeddings concatenated with type embeddings and fed through 12 transformer layers. Pretrained with ITM and MLM, this monolithic design yields large efficiency gains (e.g., ~15 ms vs. ~900 ms for region-feature models; ≥4× faster than grid-feature models) while achieving competitive VQAv2, NLVR2, and retrieval performance.

6. [MULTIMODAL] Multi-Modal Hallucination Control by Visual Information Grounding (2403.14003)
   problem: Autoregressive vision-language models (VLMs) often hallucinate objects or attributes because the model’s reliance on the visual prompt fades as more tokens are generated, and there is no principled, annotation-free way to quantify this grounding loss.
   method:  Introduce a visual Prompt Dependency Measure (PDM) that compares the conditioned distribution p(y_t | y_<t, x, c) to the unconditioned language-only distribution p(y_t | y_<t, x) using distances such as the Hellinger metric (PDM-H). This quantifies how much a token depends on the image versus the language prior. Empirically, PDM decreases with token position (conditioning dilution/fading memory), and lower PDM correlates with increased hallucinated objects in captions, while also identifying ‘contextual pressure’ cases (e.g., function words) where low PDM does not imply hallucination.

7. [LLM_NLP] Tailor: Generating and Perturbing Text with Semantic Controls (2107.07150)
   problem: Controlled text perturbation for evaluation and robustness typically requires task-specific models or sparse controls that don’t specify how to realize targeted edits, making them costly and hard to generalize.
   method:  Tailor finetunes a pretrained seq2seq model (T5) to follow structured control codes derived from PropBank SRL, covering three control types: semantic roles, keyword content with specificity (complete/partial/sparse), and span ordering via predicate form and blank positions. Inputs use a bracketed header of controls plus a context with blanks; outputs explicitly tag generated spans, decoupling semantic intent from surface placement and enabling strict or flexible edits by masking subsets of arguments. To enforce adherence to controls, Tailor adds unlikelihood training with negative samples created by perturbing roles, lemmas, and specificities, yielding higher controllability and closeness than MLE alone.

8. [CV] Overload: Latency Attacks on Object Detection for Edge Devices (2304.05370)
   problem: Existing adversarial attacks on object detectors largely target accuracy, or when targeting latency, rely on complex IoU-based objectives that are computationally and memory intensive—especially problematic on edge devices. The key open question is which model factors most effectively increase inference time with minimal attack cost.
   method:  Overload analyzes NMS time complexity and shows latency is dominated by the number of objects fed into NMS rather than the number of surviving boxes. Based on this, it formulates a simple white-box objective that maximizes per-detection objectness confidence and class probabilities via a monotonic increasing function, inflating the number of pre-NMS objects without tracking pairwise IoUs. The attack is optimized with PGD-style updates under a small perturbation budget and is NMS-agnostic. Empirically, it generates >20k ghost objects and increases end-to-end latency by ~11–13× on Jetson NX, while using less memory/time than Daedalus and Phantom Sponge (e.g., ~3 min for 16 images at 7.5 GB vs. ~145 min for 10 images at 8.5 GB).

9. [RL] Stabilizing Off-Policy Deep Reinforcement Learning from Pixels (2207.00986)
   problem: Off-policy TD-learning from pixel observations is highly unstable: critics quickly overfit to their own noisy targets and fail to learn from sparse, low-magnitude rewards, and it is unclear why image augmentations help.
   method:  The paper diagnoses a 'visual deadly triad'—exclusive reliance on TD-loss, unregularized CNN encoders, and low-magnitude sparse rewards—showing it induces catastrophic self-overfitting. Using offline policy-evaluation on fixed transitions, the authors demonstrate near-perfect correlation to target Q-values but poor correlation to Monte-Carlo returns without augmentations, and that overfitting is tied to high-frequency, spatially inconsistent CNN features. They introduce the Normalized Discontinuity (ND) score to quantify feature/gradient discontinuities, show ND strongly correlates with performance in online and offline training, and explain random shift augmentations as implicit gradient smoothing through shifted computation graphs.

1. When Does Contrastive Learning Preserve Adversarial Robustness from Pretraining to Finetuning? (Neural Information Processing Systems 2021, cites: 141)
   Contrastive learning (CL) can learn generalizable feature representations and achieve the state-of-the-art performance of downstream tasks by finetuning a linear classifier on top of it. However, as adversarial robustness becomes vital in image classification, it remains unclear whether or not CL is able to preserve robustness to downstream tasks. The main challenge is that in the self-supervised pretraining + supervised finetuning paradigm, adversarial robustness is easily forgotten due to a learning task mismatch from pretraining to finetuning. We call such a challenge 'cross-task robustness transferability'. To address the above problem, in this paper we revisit and advance CL principles through the lens of robustness enhancement. We show that (1) the design of contrastive views matters: High-frequency components of images are beneficial to improving model robustness; (2) Augmenting CL with pseudo-supervision stimulus (e.g., resorting to feature clustering) helps preserve robustness without forgetting. Equipped with our new designs, we propose AdvCL, a novel adversarial contrastive pretraining framework. We show that AdvCL is able to enhance cross-task robustness transferability without loss of model accuracy and finetuning efficiency. With a thorough experimental study, we demonstrate that AdvCL outperforms the state-of-the-art self-supervised robust learning methods across multiple datasets (CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and STL-10) and finetuning schemes (linear evaluation and full model finetuning).

2. Counterfactual contrastive learning: robust representations via causal image synthesis (DEMI@MICCAI 2024, cites: 12)
   Contrastive pretraining is well-known to improve downstream task performance and model generalisation, especially in limited label settings. However, it is sensitive to the choice of augmentation pipeline. Positive pairs should preserve semantic information while destroying domain-specific information. Standard augmentation pipelines emulate domain-specific changes with pre-defined photometric transformations, but what if we could simulate realistic domain changes instead? In this work, we show how to utilise recent progress in counterfactual image generation to this effect. We propose CF-SimCLR, a counterfactual contrastive learning approach which leverages approximate counterfactual inference for positive pair creation. Comprehensive evaluation across five datasets, on chest radiography and mammography, demonstrates that CF-SimCLR substantially improves robustness to acquisition shift with higher downstream performance on both in- and out-of-distribution data, particularly for domains which are under-represented during training.

In this paper, we study the adversarial robustness of AI-generated image detectors, focusing on Contrastive LanguageImage Pretraining (CLIP)-based methods that rely on Visual Transformer (ViT) backbones and comparing their performance with CNN-based methods. We study the robustness to different adversarial attacks under a variety of conditions and analyze both numerical results and frequency-domain patterns. CLIPbased detectors are found to be vulnerable to white-box attacks justlike CNN-based detectors. However, attacks do not easily transfer between CNN-based and CLIP-based methods. Overall, this analysis provides new insights into the properties of forensic detectors that can help to develop more effective strategies.

   Contrastive pretraining can substantially increase model generalisation and downstream performance. However, the quality of the learned representations is highly dependent on the data augmentation strategy applied to generate positive pairs. Positive contrastive pairs should preserve semantic meaning while discarding unwanted variations related to the data acquisition domain. Traditional contrastive pipelines attempt to simulate domain shifts through pre-defined generic image transformations. However, these do not always mimic realistic and relevant domain variations for medical imaging, such as scanner differences. To tackle this issue, we herein introduce counterfactual contrastive learning, a novel framework leveraging recent advances in causal image synthesis to create contrastive positive pairs that faithfully capture relevant domain variations. Our method, evaluated across five datasets encompassing both chest radiography and mammography data, for two established contrastive objectives (SimCLR and DINO-v2), outperforms standard contrastive learning in terms of robustness to acquisition shift. Notably, counterfactual contrastive learning achieves superior downstream performance on both in-distribution and external datasets, especially for images acquired with scanners under-represented in the training set. The fine-tuned CLIP model achieved an accuracy of 43.5% on adversarially perturbed images, compared to only 4% for the baseline model. To alleviate distortion of adversarial image embeddings of noisy web images, we further propose to regularise AdvFLYP by penalising deviation of adversarial image features. We show that logit- and feature-level regularisation terms benefit robustness and clean accuracy, respectively. 1. Air Pollutant Concentration Prediction Using a Generative Adversarial Network with Multi-Scale Convolutional Long Short-Term Memory and Enhanced U-Net (Sustainability 2025, cites: 0)
   Accurate prediction of air pollutant concentrations, particularly fine particulate matter (PM2.5), is essential for controlling and preventing heavy pollution incidents by providing early warnings of harmful substances in the atmosphere. This study proposes a novel spatiotemporal model for PM2.5 concentration prediction based on a Conditional Wasserstein Generative Adversarial Network with Gradient Penalty (CWGAN-GP). The framework incorporates three key design components: First, the generator employs an Inception-style Convolutional Long Short-Term Memory (ConvLSTM) network, integrating parallel multi-scale convolutions and hierarchical normalization. This design enhances multi-scale spatiotemporal feature extraction while effectively suppressing boundary artifacts via a map-masking layer. Second, the discriminator adopts an architecturally enhanced U-Net, incorporating spectral normalization and shallow instance normalization. Feature-guided masked skip connections are introduced, and the output is designed as a raw score map to mitigate premature saturation during training. Third, a composite loss function is utilized, combining adversarial loss, feature-matching loss, and inter-frame spatiotemporal smoothness. A sliding-window conditioning mechanism is also implemented, leveraging multi-level features from the discriminator for joint spatiotemporal optimization. Experiments conducted on multi-source gridded data from Dongguan demonstrate that the model achieves a 12 h prediction performance with a Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) of 4.61 μg/m3, a Mean Absolute Error (MAE) of 6.42 μg/m3, and a Coefficient of Determination (R2) of 0.80. The model significantly alleviates performance degradation in long-term predictions when the forecast horizon is extended from 3 to 12 h, the RMSE increases by only 1.84 μg/m3, and regional deviations remain within ±3 μg/m3. These results indicate strong capabilities in spatial topology reconstruction and robustness against concentration anomalies, highlighting the model’s potential for hyperlocal air quality early warning. It should be noted that the empirical validation is limited to the specific environmental conditions of Dongguan, and the model’s generalizability to other geographical and climatic settings requires further investigation.

2. Towards More Robust Interpretation via Local Gradient Alignment (AAAI Conference on Artificial Intelligence 2022, cites: 8)
   Neural network interpretation methods, particularly feature attribution methods, are known to be fragile with respect to adversarial input perturbations. 
To address this, several methods for enhancing the local smoothness of the gradient while training have been proposed for attaining robust feature attributions.
However, the lack of considering the normalization of the attributions, which is essential in their visualizations, has been an obstacle to understanding and improving the robustness of feature attribution methods. 
In this paper, we provide new insights by taking such normalization into account. First, we show that for every non-negative homogeneous neural network, a naive l2-robust criterion for gradients is not normalization invariant, which means that two functions with the same normalized gradient can have different values. 
Second, we formulate a normalization invariant cosine distance-based criterion and derive its upper bound, which gives insight for why simply minimizing the Hessian norm at the input, as has been done in previous work, is not sufficient for attaining robust feature attribution. Finally, we propose to combine both l2 and cosine distance-based criteria as regularization terms to leverage the advantages of both in aligning the local gradient. As a result, we experimentally show that models trained with our method produce much more robust interpretations on CIFAR-10 and ImageNet-100 without significantly hurting the accuracy, compared to the recent baselines. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first work to verify the robustness of interpretation on a larger-scale dataset beyond CIFAR-10, thanks to the computational efficiency of our method.

Detection performance is assessed using classical and ensemble learning methods (SVM (Support Vector Machines), RF (Random Forest), XGBoost (Extreme Gradient Boosting), LightGBM (Light Gradient Boosting Machine)), unsupervised baselines (K-Means and DBSCAN (Density-Based Spatial Clustering of Applications with Noise)), and DL (Deep-Learning) anomaly detectors based on Autoencoder reconstruction and GAN (Generative Adversarial Network)-based scoring. Experimental results on UNSW-NB15 indicate that ensemble-based models provide the strongest overall detection performance, while the signal-processing augmentation and PCA-based compactness support efficient deployment in embedded contexts. The findings confirm that integrating lightweight signal processing with AI-driven models enables effective and adaptable identification of malicious network traffic supporting deployment-oriented embedded cybersecurity and motivating future real-time validation on edge hardware.

4. An improved plasma boundary detection based on an optimized U-Net deep learning network for Tokamak devices (Journal of Instrumentation 2026, cites: 0)
   Tokamak is a typical nuclear fusion device, where effective control of the plasma boundary contributes to its long-pulse stable operation. Nowadays, CCD camera diagnostic systems are widely used in most fusion devices. By detecting the CCD images of plasma discharge, the plasma boundary can be obtained. The U-Net deep learning network, with its excellent feature extraction and boundary perception capabilities, possesses excellent image detection characteristics and can be applied in boundary detection. However, automated and accurate detection of plasma boundaries from these images remains challenging due to their inherent complex characteristics, such as low contrast, blurry edges, nonuniform brightness distribution, and high inter-class similarity. To address the challenge of automated, high-precision plasma boundary detection in Tokamak devices, this paper develops a novel diagnostic method based on an optimized deep learning network. Firstly, batch normalization (BN) layers are introduced between each convolution operation and activation function in the encoder-decoder, effectively mitigating internal covariate shift during small-sample training and significantly improving training speed and gradient stability. Furthermore, by embedding the convolutional block attention module (CBAM) before each downsampling, the U-Net network is enabled to adaptively enhance the response of discriminative feature channels and key spatial regions, effectively distinguishing visually similar areas such as the plasma core, edge, and background. Additionally, a hybrid loss function combining cross-entropy and Dice loss is designed to enhance boundary sensitivity while maintaining smooth and stable training, thereby alleviating model bias caused by class imbalance. To verify the effectiveness of the detection algorithm, plasma discharge images through the CCD camera on the Thailand Tokamak-1 (TT-1) device are obtained, and a specialized dataset for plasma image detection is constructed. Experimental results demonstrate that the optimized model achieves a final mIoU of 92.42%, mPrecision of 96.67%, and mRecall of 95.24%, significantly outperforming both the original U-Net network and Canny edge detection. The proposed algorithm not only reduces misjudgment and boundary discontinuities but also exhibits stronger robustness and generalization capability, confirming its suitability for accurate plasma boundary detection.

In the second stage, we design a coarse-to-fine global optimization matching strategy. Initially, we incorporate motion smoothing constraints and improve the Fast Library for Approximate Nearest Neighbors (FLANN) algorithm by utilizing neighborhood information for coarse matching. Then, we eliminate mismatches via homography-guided Random Sample Consensus (RANSAC) and further refine the matching using the Levenberg-Marquardt (L-M) algorithm to reduce cumulative errors and achieve global optimization. Finally, multi-band blending is used to eliminate the ghosting due to unalignment and make the image transition more natural. Experiments show that the visual effect of dental panoramas generated by the proposed method is significantly better than that of other methods, addressing the problems of sparse splicing discontinuities caused by sparse keypoints, ghosting due to parallax, and distortion caused by the accumulation of errors in multi-image splicing in oral endoscopic image stitching.

6. for Prediction City Region Re-Weighting ( 2022, cites: 0)
