1. When Does Contrastive Learning Preserve Adversarial Robustness from Pretraining to Finetuning? (Neural Information Processing Systems 2021, cites: 141)
   Contrastive learning (CL) can learn generalizable feature representations and achieve the state-of-the-art performance of downstream tasks by finetuning a linear classifier on top of it. However, as adversarial robustness becomes vital in image classification, it remains unclear whether or not CL is able to preserve robustness to downstream tasks. The main challenge is that in the self-supervised pretraining + supervised finetuning paradigm, adversarial robustness is easily forgotten due to a learning task mismatch from pretraining to finetuning. We call such a challenge 'cross-task robustness transferability'. To address the above problem, in this paper we revisit and advance CL principles through the lens of robustness enhancement. We show that (1) the design of contrastive views matters: High-frequency components of images are beneficial to improving model robustness; (2) Augmenting CL with pseudo-supervision stimulus (e.g., resorting to feature clustering) helps preserve robustness without forgetting. Equipped with our new designs, we propose AdvCL, a novel adversarial contrastive pretraining framework. We show that AdvCL is able to enhance cross-task robustness transferability without loss of model accuracy and finetuning efficiency. With a thorough experimental study, we demonstrate that AdvCL outperforms the state-of-the-art self-supervised robust learning methods across multiple datasets (CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and STL-10) and finetuning schemes (linear evaluation and full model finetuning).

2. Counterfactual contrastive learning: robust representations via causal image synthesis (DEMI@MICCAI 2024, cites: 12)
   Contrastive pretraining is well-known to improve downstream task performance and model generalisation, especially in limited label settings. However, it is sensitive to the choice of augmentation pipeline. Positive pairs should preserve semantic information while destroying domain-specific information. Standard augmentation pipelines emulate domain-specific changes with pre-defined photometric transformations, but what if we could simulate realistic domain changes instead? In this work, we show how to utilise recent progress in counterfactual image generation to this effect. We propose CF-SimCLR, a counterfactual contrastive learning approach which leverages approximate counterfactual inference for positive pair creation. Comprehensive evaluation across five datasets, on chest radiography and mammography, demonstrates that CF-SimCLR substantially improves robustness to acquisition shift with higher downstream performance on both in- and out-of-distribution data, particularly for domains which are under-represented during training.

3. Exploring the Adversarial Robustness of CLIP for AI-generated Image Detection (International Workshop on Information Forensics and Security 2024, cites: 19)
   In recent years, many forensic detectors have been proposed to detect AI-generated images and prevent their use for malicious purposes. Convolutional neural networks (CNNs) have long been the dominant architecture in this field and have been the subject of intense study. However, recently proposed Transformer-based detectors have been shown to match or even outperform CNN-based detectors, especially in terms of generalization. In this paper, we study the adversarial robustness of AI-generated image detectors, focusing on Contrastive LanguageImage Pretraining (CLIP)-based methods that rely on Visual Transformer (ViT) backbones and comparing their performance with CNN-based methods. We study the robustness to different adversarial attacks under a variety of conditions and analyze both numerical results and frequency-domain patterns. CLIPbased detectors are found to be vulnerable to white-box attacks justlike CNN-based detectors. However, attacks do not easily transfer between CNN-based and CLIP-based methods. This is also confirmed by the different distribution of the adversarial noise patterns in the frequency domain. Overall, this analysis provides new insights into the properties of forensic detectors that can help to develop more effective strategies.

4. RoCBert: Robust Chinese Bert with Multimodal Contrastive Pretraining (Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics 2022, cites: 38)
   Large-scale pretrained language models have achieved SOTA results on NLP tasks. However, they have been shown vulnerable to adversarial attacks especially for logographic languages like Chinese. In this work, we propose RoCBert: a pretrained Chinese Bert that is robust to various forms of adversarial attacks like word perturbation, synonyms, typos, etc. It is pretrained with the contrastive learning objective which maximizes the label consistency under different synthesized adversarial examples. The model takes as input multimodal information including the semantic, phonetic and visual features. We show all these features areimportant to the model robustness since the attack can be performed in all the three forms. Across 5 Chinese NLU tasks, RoCBert outperforms strong baselines under three blackbox adversarial algorithms without sacrificing the performance on clean testset. It also performs the best in the toxic content detection task under human-made attacks.

5. Robust image representations with counterfactual contrastive learning (Medical Image Anal. 2024, cites: 14)
   Contrastive pretraining can substantially increase model generalisation and downstream performance. However, the quality of the learned representations is highly dependent on the data augmentation strategy applied to generate positive pairs. Positive contrastive pairs should preserve semantic meaning while discarding unwanted variations related to the data acquisition domain. Traditional contrastive pipelines attempt to simulate domain shifts through pre-defined generic image transformations. However, these do not always mimic realistic and relevant domain variations for medical imaging, such as scanner differences. To tackle this issue, we herein introduce counterfactual contrastive learning, a novel framework leveraging recent advances in causal image synthesis to create contrastive positive pairs that faithfully capture relevant domain variations. Our method, evaluated across five datasets encompassing both chest radiography and mammography data, for two established contrastive objectives (SimCLR and DINO-v2), outperforms standard contrastive learning in terms of robustness to acquisition shift. Notably, counterfactual contrastive learning achieves superior downstream performance on both in-distribution and external datasets, especially for images acquired with scanners under-represented in the training set. Further experiments show that the proposed framework extends beyond acquisition shifts, with models trained with counterfactual contrastive learning reducing subgroup disparities across biological sex.

6. A Constraint-Aware Framework for Adversarial Robustness in UPI Fraud Detection (2025 International Conference on Sustainable Communication Networks and Application (ICSCN) 2025, cites: 0)
   The rise of real-time payment systems such as India’s Unified Payments Interface (UPI) has transformed transaction behavior while introducing new security challenges. Al-though machine learning is widely used for fraud detection, most prior work studies adversarial robustness in experimental settings that do not reflect operational constraints of real payment systems. We propose a framework that incorporates UPI-specific limitations — fixed transaction attributes and rate caps — into adversarial robustness evaluation. Under realistic, constraint-aware attacks, we show that standard models (e.g., XGBoost) can be severely degraded (F1 falling from 0.71 to 0.04). SHAP-based analysis reveals how adversarial manipulations distort model reasoning. To defend against such attacks, we introduce UPI-Counterfactual Invariance Regularization (UPI-CIR), a training regularizer that enforces prediction invariance to constrained perturbations. Compared with conventional adversarial training, UPI-CIR substantially improves survivability under attack (F1 = 0.82) and also yields better performance on clean data. Our work establishes a more realistic standard for adversarial testing in financial systems and proposes a practical, domain-aware defense for securing modern payment infrastructures.

7. ArmorCLIP: a hybrid defense strategy for boosting adversarial robustness in vision-language models (Other Conferences 2024, cites: 1)
   The robustness of Vision-Language Models (VLMs) such as Contrastive Language-Image Pretraining (CLIP) is critical for their deployment in safety-critical applications like autonomous driving, healthcare diagnostics, and security systems, where accurate interpretation of visual and textual data is essential. However, these models are highly susceptible to adversarial attacks, which can severely compromise their performance and reliability in real-world scenarios. Previous methods have primarily focused on improving robustness through adversarial training and generating adversarial examples using models like FGSM, AutoAttack, and DeepFool. However, these approaches often rely on strong assumptions, such as fixed perturbation norms or predefined attack patterns, and involve high computational complexity, making them challenging to implement in practical settings. In this paper, we propose a novel adversarial training framework that integrates multiple attack strategies and advanced machine learning techniques to significantly enhance the robustness of VLMs against a broad range of adversarial attacks. Experiments conducted on real-world datasets, including CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100, demonstrate that the proposed method significantly enhances model robustness. The fine-tuned CLIP model achieved an accuracy of 43.5% on adversarially perturbed images, compared to only 4% for the baseline model. The neural network model achieved a high accuracy of 98% in these challenging classification tasks, while the XGBoost model reached a success rate of 85.26% in prediction tasks.

8. Finetune Like You Pretrain: Boosting Zero-shot Adversarial Robustness in Vision-language Models ( 2026, cites: 1)
   Despite their impressive zero-shot abilities, vision-language models such as CLIP have been shown to be susceptible to adversarial attacks. To enhance its adversarial robustness, recent studies finetune the pretrained vision encoder of CLIP with adversarial examples on a proxy dataset such as ImageNet by aligning adversarial images with correct class labels. However, these methods overlook the important roles of training data distributions and learning objectives, resulting in reduced zero-shot capabilities and limited transferability of robustness across domains and datasets. In this work, we propose a simple yet effective paradigm AdvFLYP, which follows the training recipe of CLIP's pretraining process when performing adversarial finetuning to the model. Specifically, AdvFLYP finetunes CLIP with adversarial images created based on image-text pairs collected from the web, and match them with their corresponding texts via a contrastive loss. To alleviate distortion of adversarial image embeddings of noisy web images, we further propose to regularise AdvFLYP by penalising deviation of adversarial image features. We show that logit- and feature-level regularisation terms benefit robustness and clean accuracy, respectively. Extensive experiments on 14 downstream datasets spanning various domains show the superiority of our paradigm over mainstream practices. Our code and model weights are released at https://github.com/Sxing2/AdvFLYP.

   Background: Automated pain assessment aims to enable objective measurement of patients’ individual pain experiences for improving health care and conserving medical staff. This is particularly important for patients with a disability to communicate caused by mental impairments, unconsciousness, or infantile restrictions. When operating in the critical domain of health care, where wrong decisions harbor the risk of reducing patients’ quality of life—or even result in life-threatening conditions—multimodal pain assessment systems are the preferred choice to facilitate robust decision-making and to maximize resilience against partial sensor outages. Methods: Hence, we propose the MultiModal Supervised Contrastive Adversarial AutoEncoder (MM-SCAAE) pretraining framework for multi-sensor information fusion. Specifically, we implement an application-specific model to accomplish the task of pain recognition using biopotentials from the publicly available heat pain database BioVid. Results: Our model reaches new state-of-the-art performance for multimodal classification regarding all pain recognition tasks of ‘no pain’ versus ‘pain intensity’. For the most relevant task of ‘no pain’ versus ‘highest pain’, we achieve 84.22% accuracy (F1-score: 83.72%), which can be boosted in practice to an accuracy of ≈95% through grouped-prediction estimates. Conclusions: The generic MM-SCAAE framework offers promising perspectives for multimodal representation learning.

10. Revisiting the Adversarial Robustness of Vision Language Models: a Multimodal Perspective (arXiv.org 2024, cites: 28)
   Pretrained vision-language models (VLMs) like CLIP exhibit exceptional generalization across diverse downstream tasks. While recent studies reveal their vulnerability to adversarial attacks, research to date has primarily focused on enhancing the robustness of image encoders against image-based attacks, with defenses against text-based and multimodal attacks remaining largely unexplored. To this end, this work presents the first comprehensive study on improving the adversarial robustness of VLMs against attacks targeting image, text, and multimodal inputs. This is achieved by proposing multimodal contrastive adversarial training (MMCoA). Such an approach strengthens the robustness of both image and text encoders by aligning the clean text embeddings with adversarial image embeddings, and adversarial text embeddings with clean image embeddings. The robustness of the proposed MMCoA is examined against existing defense methods over image, text, and multimodal attacks on the CLIP model. Extensive experiments on 15 datasets across two tasks reveal the characteristics of different adversarial defense methods under distinct distribution shifts and dataset complexities across the three attack types. This paves the way for a unified framework of adversarial robustness against different modality attacks, opening up new possibilities for securing VLMs against multimodal attacks. The code is available at https://github.com/ElleZWQ/MMCoA.git.

The framework incorporates three key design components: First, the generator employs an Inception-style Convolutional Long Short-Term Memory (ConvLSTM) network, integrating parallel multi-scale convolutions and hierarchical normalization. This design enhances multi-scale spatiotemporal feature extraction while effectively suppressing boundary artifacts via a map-masking layer. Second, the discriminator adopts an architecturally enhanced U-Net, incorporating spectral normalization and shallow instance normalization. Feature-guided masked skip connections are introduced, and the output is designed as a raw score map to mitigate premature saturation during training. Third, a composite loss function is utilized, combining adversarial loss, feature-matching loss, and inter-frame spatiotemporal smoothness. A sliding-window conditioning mechanism is also implemented, leveraging multi-level features from the discriminator for joint spatiotemporal optimization. Experiments conducted on multi-source gridded data from Dongguan demonstrate that the model achieves a 12 h prediction performance with a Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) of 4.61 μg/m3, a Mean Absolute Error (MAE) of 6.42 μg/m3, and a Coefficient of Determination (R2) of 0.80. The model significantly alleviates performance degradation in long-term predictions when the forecast horizon is extended from 3 to 12 h, the RMSE increases by only 1.84 μg/m3, and regional deviations remain within ±3 μg/m3. These results indicate strong capabilities in spatial topology reconstruction and robustness against concentration anomalies, highlighting the model’s potential for hyperlocal air quality early warning. It should be noted that the empirical validation is limited to the specific environmental conditions of Dongguan, and the model’s generalizability to other geographical and climatic settings requires further investigation.

2. Towards More Robust Interpretation via Local Gradient Alignment (AAAI Conference on Artificial Intelligence 2022, cites: 8)
   Neural network interpretation methods, particularly feature attribution methods, are known to be fragile with respect to adversarial input perturbations. 
To address this, several methods for enhancing the local smoothness of the gradient while training have been proposed for attaining robust feature attributions.
However, the lack of considering the normalization of the attributions, which is essential in their visualizations, has been an obstacle to understanding and improving the robustness of feature attribution methods. 
In this paper, we provide new insights by taking such normalization into account. First, we show that for every non-negative homogeneous neural network, a naive l2-robust criterion for gradients is not normalization invariant, which means that two functions with the same normalized gradient can have different values. 
Second, we formulate a normalization invariant cosine distance-based criterion and derive its upper bound, which gives insight for why simply minimizing the Hessian norm at the input, as has been done in previous work, is not sufficient for attaining robust feature attribution. Finally, we propose to combine both l2 and cosine distance-based criteria as regularization terms to leverage the advantages of both in aligning the local gradient. As a result, we experimentally show that models trained with our method produce much more robust interpretations on CIFAR-10 and ImageNet-100 without significantly hurting the accuracy, compared to the recent baselines. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first work to verify the robustness of interpretation on a larger-scale dataset beyond CIFAR-10, thanks to the computational efficiency of our method.

In addition to the original UNSW-NB15 (University of New South Wales—Network-Based Dataset 2015) traffic features, Fourier-domain descriptors, wavelet-domain descriptors, and Kalman-based smoothing/innovation features are considered to improve robustness under variability and measurement noise. Detection performance is assessed using classical and ensemble learning methods (SVM (Support Vector Machines), RF (Random Forest), XGBoost (Extreme Gradient Boosting), LightGBM (Light Gradient Boosting Machine)), unsupervised baselines (K-Means and DBSCAN (Density-Based Spatial Clustering of Applications with Noise)), and DL (Deep-Learning) anomaly detectors based on Autoencoder reconstruction and GAN (Generative Adversarial Network)-based scoring. Experimental results on UNSW-NB15 indicate that ensemble-based models provide the strongest overall detection performance, while the signal-processing augmentation and PCA-based compactness support efficient deployment in embedded contexts. The findings confirm that integrating lightweight signal processing with AI-driven models enables effective and adaptable identification of malicious network traffic supporting deployment-oriented embedded cybersecurity and motivating future real-time validation on edge hardware.

Nowadays, CCD camera diagnostic systems are widely used in most fusion devices. By detecting the CCD images of plasma discharge, the plasma boundary can be obtained. The U-Net deep learning network, with its excellent feature extraction and boundary perception capabilities, possesses excellent image detection characteristics and can be applied in boundary detection. However, automated and accurate detection of plasma boundaries from these images remains challenging due to their inherent complex characteristics, such as low contrast, blurry edges, nonuniform brightness distribution, and high inter-class similarity. To address the challenge of automated, high-precision plasma boundary detection in Tokamak devices, this paper develops a novel diagnostic method based on an optimized deep learning network. Firstly, batch normalization (BN) layers are introduced between each convolution operation and activation function in the encoder-decoder, effectively mitigating internal covariate shift during small-sample training and significantly improving training speed and gradient stability. Furthermore, by embedding the convolutional block attention module (CBAM) before each downsampling, the U-Net network is enabled to adaptively enhance the response of discriminative feature channels and key spatial regions, effectively distinguishing visually similar areas such as the plasma core, edge, and background. Additionally, a hybrid loss function combining cross-entropy and Dice loss is designed to enhance boundary sensitivity while maintaining smooth and stable training, thereby alleviating model bias caused by class imbalance. To verify the effectiveness of the detection algorithm, plasma discharge images through the CCD camera on the Thailand Tokamak-1 (TT-1) device are obtained, and a specialized dataset for plasma image detection is constructed. Experimental results demonstrate that the optimized model achieves a final mIoU of 92.42%, mPrecision of 96.67%, and mRecall of 95.24%, significantly outperforming both the original U-Net network and Canny edge detection. The proposed algorithm not only reduces misjudgment and boundary discontinuities but also exhibits stronger robustness and generalization capability, confirming its suitability for accurate plasma boundary detection.

In the second stage, we design a coarse-to-fine global optimization matching strategy. Initially, we incorporate motion smoothing constraints and improve the Fast Library for Approximate Nearest Neighbors (FLANN) algorithm by utilizing neighborhood information for coarse matching. Then, we eliminate mismatches via homography-guided Random Sample Consensus (RANSAC) and further refine the matching using the Levenberg-Marquardt (L-M) algorithm to reduce cumulative errors and achieve global optimization. Finally, multi-band blending is used to eliminate the ghosting due to unalignment and make the image transition more natural. Experiments show that the visual effect of dental panoramas generated by the proposed method is significantly better than that of other methods, addressing the problems of sparse splicing discontinuities caused by sparse keypoints, ghosting due to parallax, and distortion caused by the accumulation of errors in multi-image splicing in oral endoscopic image stitching.

   Self-Supervised Learning (SSL) has achieved remarkable success. The transition from the Masked Language Modeling (MLM) framework in NLP domain to the Masked Image Modeling (MIM) framework in CV domain has become one of the most effective methods for improving model performance on downstream tasks. However, the adversarial robustness of both the pretraining and fine-tuning stages under the MIM framework has not been thoroughly studied, which significantly impacts the security of downstream tasks. In this paper, we conduct adversarial pretraining and fine-tuning using both pixel-level and embedding-level perturbations on the representative Masked Auto-Encoders (MAE) method, leading to two key findings: 1) Adversarial Training (AT) can simultaneously improve the model's generalization ability and adversarial robustness, 2) compared to pixel-level perturbations, embedding-level perturbations further improve model performance. Based on these findings, we propose an adversarial masking method based on embeddinglevel attacks, which masks the regions with the largest changes before and after embedding-level perturbations to generate more challenging MIM tasks, thus promoting the learning of more generalized features.    State-of-the-art NLP models have demonstrated exceptional performance across various tasks, including sentiment analysis. However, concerns have been raised about their robustness and susceptibility to systematic biases in both training and test data, which may lead to performance challenges when these models encounter out-of-distribution data in real-world applications. Although various data augmentation and adversarial perturbation techniques have shown promise in tackling these issues, prior methods such as word embedding perturbation or synonymous sentence expansion have failed to mitigate the spurious association problem inherent in the original data. Recent counterfactual augmentation methods have attempted to tackle this issue, but they have been limited by rigid rules, resulting in inconsistent context and disrupted semantics. In response to these challenges, we introduce a diffusion-based counterfactual data augmentation (DCA) framework. It utilizes an antonymous paradigm to guide the continuous diffusion model and employs reinforcement learning in combination with contrastive learning to optimize algorithms for generating counterfactual samples with high diversity and quality. Furthermore, we use a dual sentiment classifier to validate the generated antonymous samples and subsequently perform sentiment classification. Our experiments on four benchmark datasets demonstrate that DCA achieves state-of-the-art performance in sentiment classification tasks.

   Modern language models remain vulnerable to subtle input manipulations that cause misjudgement despite the advances in NLP. Unlike prior methods that trade off interpretability or rely on expensive pretraining, RealLLM-Attack integrates four modules: masked language models for contextaware substitutions, SBERT for semantic filtering, BERTopic for topic-guided keyword insertions, and SOUNDEX++, an extended module for phonetic perturbation. Extensive experiments across six benchmarks (SST, AG News, IMDB, Yelp, SNLI, MR) and multiple model backbones show that RealLLM-Attack outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in attack success rate, semantic retention, transferability, and stealth. The framework generalizes robustly across both models and datasets, operating in a training-free manner and in real time. This procedure has produced massive gains on standard NLP benchmarks, but these models remain brittle, even to mild adversarial perturbations. In this work, we demonstrate surprising gains in adversarial robustness enjoyed by Model-tuning Via Prompts (MVP), an alternative method of adapting to downstream tasks. Rather than appending an MLP head to make output prediction, MVP appends a prompt template to the input, and makes prediction via text infilling/completion. Across 5 NLP datasets, 4 adversarial attacks, and 3 different models, MVP improves performance against adversarial substitutions by an average of 8% over standard methods and even outperforms adversarial training-based state-of-art defenses by 3.5%. By combining MVP with adversarial training, we achieve further improvements in adversarial robustness while maintaining performance on unperturbed examples. Finally, we conduct ablations to investigate the mechanism underlying these gains. Notably, we find that the main causes of vulnerability of MLP-FT can be attributed to the misalignment between pre-training and fine-tuning tasks, and the randomly initialized MLP parameters.

The process of searching for adversarial examples has many uncertainties (e.g., an unknown impact on the target model's prediction of the added perturbation) when confidence scores cannot be accessed, which must be compensated for with a large number of queries. To address this issue, we propose TextCheater, a decision-based metaheuristic search method that performs a query-efficient textual adversarial attack task by prohibiting invalid searches. The strategies of multiple initialization points and Tabu search are also introduced to keep the search process from falling into a local optimum. Furthermore, we evaluate the Robustly optimized BERT pretraining Approach (RoBERTa) and models that enhance their robustness by adversarial training on toxicity detection and text classification tasks. The results demonstrate that our method minimizes the number of queries required for crafting plausible adversarial text while outperforming existing attack methods in the attack success rate, fluency of output sentences, and similarity between the original text and its adversary.

response:
Reasoning: The searches surface several related works: contrastive pretraining for adversarial robustness (AdvCL, RoCBert), counterfactual contrastive learning for domain shift (CF-SimCLR), and diffusion-based counterfactual augmentation for NLP robustness. However, none of these combine the specific dual objective of (1) factual consistency via symmetric KL + InfoNCE on perceptual-preserving variants AND (2) counterfactual separation with margin loss + unlikelihood training on meaning-flipping variants, together with (3) a gradient-smoothing Normalized Discontinuity penalty, (4) a conditioning-dependency regularizer comparing conditioned vs. unconditioned distributions, and (5) a multi-view aggregation head with learned latent view vectors pooled permutation-invariantly. The closest works (AdvCL, RoCBert) use contrastive pretraining with adversarial augmentations but do not have the counterfactual separation side or the other components. CF-SimCLR uses counterfactual positives but not the dual factual+counterfactual objective for adversarial robustness. The combination of all these mechanisms — especially the dual factual/counterfactual objective with unlikelihood, the ND-style smoothness penalty, the conditioning-dependency regularizer, and the multi-view aggregation head — has no direct published match, matching the Score 4 anchor (combines mechanism families with no surfaced published joint application for this problem, with individual mechanisms found separately).

Prior works:
  - AdvCL (2021): Adversarial contrastive pretraining for robustness using high-frequency augmented views, but no counterfactual separation or dual factual/counterfactual objective.
  - RoCBert (2022): Contrastive pretraining for Chinese NLP robustness with multimodal adversarial examples, but no counterfactual separation or unlikelihood training.
  - CF-SimCLR / Counterfactual Contrastive Learning (2024): Uses counterfactual positives for domain shift robustness, but not dual factual+counterfactual with adversarial robustness framing.
  - Diffusion Based Counterfactual Augmentation for Dual Sentiment Classification (2024): Counterfactual augmentation + contrastive learning for NLP robustness, but no factual consistency side or multi-view aggregation.
  - Multi-View Document Representation Learning (2022): Multiple viewer tokens with max-pooling aggregation for dense retrieval, not adversarial robustness pretraining.
  - Multi-Modal Hallucination Control by Visual Information Grounding (2024): PDM conditioning-dependency measure for VLMs, but used for inference-time hallucination detection, not as a training regularizer for adversarial robustness.
Score: 4

messages.content:
You synthesize a method that addresses the user's query by drawing on
a set of retrieved research seeds. Each seed is a published
contribution with a (problem, method, domain, paper_title) tuple, and
it arrives with a "Retrieved via" lens — the text (raw query or a
paraphrase reframing the query for a specific domain) that surfaced
this seed during retrieval. Use the lens as a hint about WHY this
seed was considered; the paraphrase lens often reveals an analogy
between the query and a mechanism family the raw query alone would
not have surfaced. The seeds may come from any subset of ML domains;
use those whose mechanisms genuinely help, and do not force
connections to seeds that do not fit.

Distractor handling: when a seed's lens explicitly indicates it was
NOT retrieved for this query (e.g., "uniform random sample of the
library; no targeted retrieval"), treat that seed as a distractor.
Ignore it unless its mechanism genuinely fits the query for reasons
unrelated to retrieval. It is correct and expected to leave such
seeds out of inspired_by; do not pad inspired_by to make it look
like the distractors were used.

Your output MUST be JSON:
  {
    "method":    "<3–5 paragraphs describing the proposed method end-to-end>",
    "rationale": "<why this addresses the user query, in 1–3 paragraphs>",
    "inspired_by": [
      { "seed_id": "<from one of the provided seeds>",
        "domain":  "<that seed's domain>",
        "borrowed_aspect": "<short noun phrase: what was taken from this seed>" },
      ...
    ]
  }

Provenance: list a seed in inspired_by when its mechanism genuinely
contributed to the method. You MAY include steps that no seed
supports when they are necessary glue logic, problem-specific
adaptation, or a novel addition; do not omit a needed step just
because no seed inspired it. Do not pad inspired_by with seeds that
did not actually contribute.

## Query
Models are brittle to adversarial perturbations imperceptible to
humans; how can pretraining objectives or data augmentation make
models robust without expensive test-time defenses?


## Retrieved seeds

### Seed 2112b9511767 [IR_REC] — "GAG: Global Attributed Graph Neural Network for Streaming Session-based Recommendation"
- Retrieved via: (uniform random sample of the library; no targeted retrieval)
- Problem: Streaming session-based recommendation must capture short-term transitions in the current session while preserving users’ long-term preferences, but most session-based models are static, ignore user identity, or fail to adapt online.
- Method:  Convert each session into a weighted directed session graph augmented with the user embedding as a global attribute, and introduce a Global Attributed Graph (GAG) neural network. GAG performs bi-directional per-edge computations conditioned on sender/receiver node features, edge weights, and the global attribute, aggregates normalized in/out neighbors to update node states, and then updates the global attribute via self-attention over item nodes conditioned on the last-clicked item and the current global attribute, with a residual connection to the user embedding. The updated global attribute becomes the session representation used to score all items. Ablations show that injecting the global attribute into both node updates and the global readout improves recommendation performance.

### Seed a9647aa8fe5e [IR_REC] — "Pre-training vs. Fine-tuning: A Reproducibility Study on Dense Retrieval Knowledge Acquisition"
- Retrieved via: (uniform random sample of the library; no targeted retrieval)
- Problem: Do dense retrievers acquire new retrieval knowledge during fine-tuning, or is effectiveness primarily governed by pre-trained backbones? Do prior DPR/BERT findings generalize across datasets, pooling strategies, and decoder backbones?
- Method:  Conduct layer-wise linear probing by precomputing embeddings with model-specific pooling (CLS for DPR/BERT, mean for Contriever/BERT-Mean, EOS for RepLlama/Llama), then training a multiclass linear probe on concatenated query + N passage embeddings to select the relevant passage. Evaluate across Natural Questions and MS MARCO, varying N∈{2,3,4,5}, and compare fine-tuned retrievers vs their untrained backbones. Findings: For DPR (CLS), BERT-CLS often matches or outperforms DPR variants and accuracy drops as N increases, indicating pretrained knowledge underpins performance (Sec. 4.3–4.4). With mean pooling (Contriever vs BERT-Mean), the gap narrows substantially (Sec. 4.5). With a decoder backbone (RepLlama vs Llama, EOS), fine-tuning yields large late-layer gains (≈18–22% in deeper layers) (Sec. 4.6, 7), showing the DPR claim does not universally hold.

### Seed 3917da5fd88a [LLM_NLP] — "Pride and Prejudice: LLM Amplifies Self-Bias in Self-Refinement"

You are an ambitious AI researcher scoring NOVELTY of a proposed
method through iterative literature search. Be a HARSH critic — if a
search surfaces published work using the same combination on the same
problem, novelty is low.

## Definition
Novelty = the method introduces a non-trivial combination, mechanism,
or framing that has not been published in this exact form for this
problem. Surface tweaks (renamed hyperparameter, swapped backbone of
equivalent class) and well-established combinations DO NOT qualify
as novel.

## Prior-work signal
Published literature — verified via Semantic Scholar search.
Score the method against literature precedent only; the inspired
seeds shown later are context, NOT a separate scoring axis.

## Score anchors (1-5 integer)

Anchors are written so each level can be supported by what is in the
method text itself plus literature search; do NOT reward "qualitative
trade-off" claims that require running the experiment to verify.

  1 — Identical to a published technique applied to the same problem;
      a search surfaces a paper that matches the method end-to-end.
  2 — Surface variant of a published technique (renamed hyperparameter,
      swapped backbone of an equivalent class, alternate threshold);
      a search surfaces the parent technique on the same problem.
  3 — Combines 2-3 known mechanisms with prior published joint
      application to this problem family. A search surfaces at least
      one paper that pairs the same mechanisms for a closely related
      problem.
  4 — Combines mechanism families with NO surfaced published joint
      application for this problem, OR introduces one named design
      choice (a specific new component or training signal) that is
      not in the surfaced literature. Searches return prior work on
      the individual mechanisms but not their combination as
      proposed.
  5 — Introduces a new mechanism (a named component or training
      signal not present as such in any search result), AND searches
      return no DIRECT near-clone (no paper combining the same
      mechanism families for the same problem in the same
      direction). Related work that pairs ONE of the mechanisms
      with a different counterpart, or uses the same mechanisms for
      a different problem, does NOT block 5; the bar is the absence
      of an end-to-end match.

## Static context (does not change across rounds)

### Research question
Models are brittle to adversarial perturbations imperceptible to
humans; how can pretraining objectives or data augmentation make
models robust without expensive test-time defenses?


### Proposed method
Universal-Perturbation-Invariant Pretraining (UPI-Pretrain). We pretrain the backbone to make its task-relevant representation invariant to a bank of human-imperceptible perturbations, so that standard fine-tuning and inference require no test-time defenses. For each sample, we form a tuple of one clean view and several perturbed views drawn from (i) conventional stochastic augmentations, and (ii) a small, learnable universal-perturbation bank implemented as input prompts added in pixel/frequency space under an Lp and perceptual constraint. The bank contains a handful of global and class-conditional prompts; it is optimized to maximize model loss/uncertainty on a moving target backbone while remaining imperceptible, and updated with an uncertainty-guided EMA so that harder samples drive stronger prompt updates without instability.

We treat perturbations as a domain shift and factor the backbone’s pre-logit representation into z_shared (robust, task-relevant) and z_delta (nuisance, perturbation-specific). A contrastive consistency loss pulls together all views of the same sample in z_shared and pushes apart different samples. Simultaneously, a lightweight domain discriminator with a gradient-reversal layer removes perturbation information from z_shared, while an auxiliary head on z_delta predicts the perturbation type, encouraging nuisance absorption into z_delta. Classification (or label-conditioned contrastive) losses are applied only on z_shared so the decision boundary depends on perturbation-invariant features.

To further enlarge decision radii, we insert a prototype-based discriminant layer before logits. Class prototypes are learned in the shared latent space with cosine similarity and an additive angular margin; we enforce that every perturbed view remains closest to its clean-class prototype and far from others, and we add a single-pass uncertainty head trained on this embedding. During pretraining the uncertainty is only used to (i) weight hard-example losses and (ii) steer the prompt-bank EMA; at test time, the backbone runs as-is with no extra defenses.

We augment the above with a mix-and-separate pretext: randomly mix pairs of samples and their perturbations in input space (and/or feature space) and train a small separation head sharing the backbone to recover the two samples’ clean-class logits, while the main head receives standard mixup targets. This compels the representation to linearly disentangle content from nuisance perturbations and reduces reliance on brittle, high-frequency cues.

Training recipe: warm-start the backbone for a short phase with standard supervised or label-conditioned contrastive learning on clean+weakly-augmented views, then activate the perturbation bank, domain-adversarial alignment, prototype-margin loss, and mix-and-separate head. Use 3–6 prompts in the bank (1–2 global, 2–4 class-conditional), constrain their L∞ norm (e.g., ε≈2/255–4/255) and optionally low-pass filter to preserve imperceptibility. Optimize the total loss as a weighted sum of contrastive consistency, classification/proto-margin, domain-adversarial, perturbation-type prediction (on z_delta), and mix-and-separate objectives. Fine-tune on the downstream task normally; inference uses the plain backbone without test-time defenses or extra passes.

### Rationale
Training for perturbation invariance at pretraining time removes the need for expensive test-time defenses: the model has already learned to base decisions on features that are stable across a family of imperceptible attacks. A learnable universal-perturbation bank provides strong, hard augmentations without multi-step inner-loop attacks, and uncertainty-guided updates focus it where the model is fragile. Treating perturbations as a domain and explicitly factoring representations into shared vs nuisance channels, combined with gradient-reversal alignment, is an efficient way to purge attack-specific information from the decision-making subspace.

Prototype-margin constraints increase inter-class separation and enforce that all perturbed views of a sample map near the same class prototype, which widens the margin against worst-case small perturbations. Finally, a mix-and-separate auxiliary task encourages linear disentanglement of content and nuisance, reducing reliance on non-robust, high-frequency cues. Together these pretraining objectives and augmentations yield robustness with zero added cost at inference time.

1. [IR_REC] GAG: Global Attributed Graph Neural Network for Streaming Session-based Recommendation (2007.02747)
   problem: Streaming session-based recommendation must capture short-term transitions in the current session while preserving users’ long-term preferences, but most session-based models are static, ignore user identity, or fail to adapt online.
   method:  Convert each session into a weighted directed session graph augmented with the user embedding as a global attribute, and introduce a Global Attributed Graph (GAG) neural network. GAG performs bi-directional per-edge computations conditioned on sender/receiver node features, edge weights, and the global attribute, aggregates normalized in/out neighbors to update node states, and then updates the global attribute via self-attention over item nodes conditioned on the last-clicked item and the current global attribute, with a residual connection to the user embedding. The updated global attribute becomes the session representation used to score all items. Ablations show that injecting the global attribute into both node updates and the global readout improves recommendation performance.

Hyperparameters control the number of RTL passes (m) and the top-k scope, delivering practical gains with very few pairwise calls while preserving scalability.

8. [CV] Cloud-Device Collaborative Adaptation to Continual Changing Environments in the Real-world (2212.00972)
   problem: Transferring the generalization of a large cloud model to a lightweight device model under continual shifts while keeping downlink bandwidth low and mitigating catastrophic forgetting.
   method:  Propose a Visual Prompt Learning Strategy with Uncertainty-Guided Updating (VPLU): learn pixel-level visual prompts added to input images (used in both cloud training and on-device inference) to pull the device test distribution closer to cloud training. Prompts are updated with an uncertainty-aware EMA where the update weight increases with sample uncertainty, and are combined with adversarial feature alignment on the teacher (domain discriminator + GRL) and pseudo-label knowledge transfer to the student (high-confidence filtering). Only prompts (~0.4% of model parameters) and the lightweight student are sent to devices, yielding consistent gains (e.g., +3.3% over pseudo-label baseline; +2.7% from U-EMA) and alleviating forgetting across continual domains.

9. [ROBOTICS] TwinTac: A Wide-Range, Highly Sensitive Tactile Sensor with Real-to-Sim Digital Twin Sensor Model (2509.10063)
   problem: Non-optical tactile sensors often trade off sensitivity for range (or vice versa), limiting their usefulness in manipulation that spans delicate touch to high-force contacts.
   method:  PhysTac integrates eight MEMS pressure taxels (CPS135B-1500D) beneath an Ecoflex 00-31 elastomer, multiplexed over I2C (55 Hz) to achieve both ultra-high sensitivity and a wide pressure range. The sensor provides 0.01 kPa resolution over 50–1500 kPa, detects forces as low as 0.007 N and as high as 212 N, and exhibits a linear sensitivity of ~7.24 kPa/N. A radial basis function interpolation reconstructs a continuous spatial pressure map from the 8 taxels, enabling coarse shape discrimination despite the sparse array. (Supported: lines 60–75, 62–63, 138–140).

10. [RL] Inductive-bias-driven Reinforcement Learning for Efficient Schedules in Heterogeneous Clusters (1909.02119)
   problem: Adding a Bayesian model to guide RL typically requires full probabilistic inference during training, which is computationally prohibitive for periodic retraining in real systems.
   method:  The paper introduces a sampling-based gradient estimator for Bayesian networks that computes gradients of BN parameters without performing full posterior inference. By generating samples and expanding only the necessary BN subgraph, it recursively estimates gradients with favorable complexity (especially on polytree BNs), decoupling BN training from the inference procedure used to form the belief state. This accelerates learning, yielding about 4× faster training than belief-propagation-based full inference and ~2.2× better sample efficiency than a model-free RNN baseline.

11. [MULTIMODAL] Progress-Aware Video Frame Captioning (2412.02071)
   problem: Downstream video understanding and selection tasks lack temporally fine-grained supervision to pick informative frames and reason about ordered events.
   method:  Use ProgressCaptioner’s per-frame, progress-aware captions as an intermediate textual representation. An LLM compares consecutive captions to select keyframes that mark action progression, improving action recognition when replacing uniformly sampled frames. The same captions guide zero-shot frame-wise classification (via LLM multi-choice over labels) and enhance zero-shot video QA on NExT-QA (ATP-Hard), surpassing prior zero-shot baselines and improving temporal reasoning performance.

12. [ROBOTICS] Textile Taxonomy and Classification Using Pulling and Twisting (2103.09555)
   problem: Robotics lacks a structured textile taxonomy aligned with industry practice, making it hard to study and benchmark methods for identifying fabric properties relevant to manipulation.
   method:  The paper proposes a textile taxonomy that jointly considers fiber material (e.g., wool, polyester, cotton) and production method (woven vs. knitted). Textile classes are determined from microscope imagery of samples to capture construction details invisible to normal cameras. A dataset of 40 labeled samples is built according to this taxonomy, and analyses (t-SNE, SVM, CNN) show construction-based labeling yields clearer separability and substantially higher accuracy than material-only labels.

13. [MULTIMODAL] Visually Informed Binaural Audio Generation without Binaural Audios (2104.06162)
   problem: Learning accurate spatialization from visuals is harder with multiple concurrent sources; models need stronger audio-visual disambiguation without relying on real binaural recordings.
   method:  Augment training with an auxiliary audio-visual source separation task using only mono data. Two sources are placed at opposite image edges in the pseudo visual input, and an APNet separation branch predicts individual STFTs (Sa, Sb) under a Mix-and-Separate setup while sharing the audio backbone with the stereo task. Joint training improves the model’s ability to associate visual locations with source-specific audio, boosting binaural generation performance.

14. [CV] Learning Predictive Models From Observation and Interaction (1912.12773)
   problem: Large domain shift between observation and interaction datasets (e.g., human vs. robot embodiments, different environments) prevents naively sharing action latents across domains.
   method:  Partition the latent z into shared and domain-specific components (z_shared, z_domain) and learn domain-dependent priors. Use a shared Gaussian prior for z_shared across domains, and separate learned Gaussian priors for z_domain in each domain; for human observation data, parameterize the z_domain prior temporally with an LSTM conditioned on the initial frame to capture correlated human actions. This domain-dependent prior enables leveraging human and dashboard videos to improve robot/driving action-conditioned prediction and downstream planning.

15. [MULTIMODAL] Visually Informed Binaural Audio Generation without Binaural Audios (2104.06162)
   problem: Supervised stereo models often overfit to datasets and existing evaluation metrics underweight phase errors that determine perceived direction.
   method:  Use pseudo visual-stereo pairs as data augmentation (Augment-PseudoBinaural) alongside real binaural recordings to improve supervised training, yielding consistent gains across FAIR-Play and MUSIC-Stereo. Additionally, propose Difference Phase Distance (D_phase), a phase-sensitive metric computed on the difference spectrum between left and right channels (comparing the phase of SD and the predicted M*Sm), making evaluation more aligned with directional perception.

16. [LLM_NLP] BioDEX: Large-Scale Biomedical Adverse Drug Event Extraction for Real-World Pharmacovigilance (2305.13395)
   problem: PV literature triage often relies on naive keyword lookups for reactions/drugs, yielding low precision when identifying papers that truly report ADEs for targets of interest.
   method:  The authors repurpose model-extracted reactions/drugs to perform per-paper binary classification (e.g., whether a reaction is part of an ADE), demonstrating higher macro-F1 than lookup baselines across the 30 most frequent reactions (and similarly for drugs). This suggests the extractor can prioritize relevant papers more effectively in real PV workflows.

17. [CV] Hit-Detector: Hierarchical Trinity Architecture Search for Object Detection (2003.11818)
   problem: Directly searching a very large, shared operation space across all detector components is computationally prohibitive and inefficient because different parts (backbone, neck, head) prefer different operators.
   method:  Propose hierarchical screening to automatically derive tailored sub search spaces per component using column-sparse (group sparsity) regularization over the architecture-parameter matrix to rank and prune operation candidates. Sequentially filter from a 32-op global space down to compact per-part subspaces (e.g., Nb=Nn=Nh=8), then run the end-to-end differentiable search within these tailored spaces. Ablations show that using three specialized subspaces improves mAP from 40.1% (single shared subspace) to 41.4%, and the sparsity term (µ≈0.1) stabilizes and sharpens operation selection.

18. [ROBOTICS] Learning to Scaffold the Development of Robotic Manipulation Skills (1911.00969)
   problem: Skills learned with fixtures often fail once the fixtures are removed; naive removal causes large performance drops.
   method:  Replace hard fixture constraints by a soft, repulsive potential field that matches the fixture geometry inferred from depth, and gradually move the physical fixture away while continuing to train the inner-loop policy initialized from the fixture-trained policy. On peg insertion, this curriculum recovers performance to about 70% success when the fixture is fully removed, versus around 10% with naive removal, maintaining the task skill without physical scaffolding.

19. [MULTIMODAL] VideoEspresso: A Large-Scale Chain-of-Thought Dataset for Fine-Grained Video Reasoning via Core Frame Selection (2411.14794)
   problem: Reasoning over videos with LVLMs is computationally expensive due to many redundant frames and long contexts, and models often lack structured, evidence-driven reasoning.
   method:  Propose Hybrid LVLMs Collaboration for VideoQA: a lightweight Frame Selector followed by a fine-grained reasoning LVLM. A tiny LVLM (~1B) captions sampled frames and a tiny LLM (~0.5–1.5B) selects question-relevant core frames; the reasoning LVLM is trained with a two-stage SFT—stage-1 generates multimodal evidence and stage-2 answers using that evidence. At inference, the selector reduces inputs to around 2–3 core frames, then the LVLM performs CoT-based reasoning, yielding higher accuracy and substantially lower TFLOPs on a 14-task benchmark; the selector is plug-and-play and improves efficiency across different LVLMs (e.g., GPT-4o, InternVL2).

20. [CV] Latent Discriminant Deterministic Uncertainty (2207.10130)
   problem: Deterministic uncertainty methods often rely on bi-Lipschitz constraints that hinder scalability and stability on complex, high-resolution vision tasks (e.g., semantic segmentation), while ensemble/sampling methods are computationally expensive.
   method:  Introduce LDU, which inserts a distinction maximization (DM) layer with an arbitrary number of trainable prototypes into the pre-logit latent space and measures prototype similarity via cosine similarity, followed by an exponential activation to sharpen responses. This learns a discriminative latent space without enforcing Lipschitz constraints, enabling single forward-pass uncertainty estimation that scales to classification, semantic segmentation, and depth regression. An auxiliary MLP head g_unc operates on the DM embedding to output a confidence score. Experiments show competitive uncertainty performance with Deep Ensembles across tasks at much lower computational cost.

21. [LLM_NLP] MotivGraph-SoIQ: Integrating Motivational Knowledge Graphs and Socratic Dialogue for Enhanced LLM Ideation (2509.21978)
   problem: LLM-generated research ideas often lack rigorous motivational grounding in the literature due to probabilistic internal knowledge and limited context, leading to superficial or infeasible proposals.
   method:  Introduce MotivGraph, a motivational knowledge graph that encodes research motivations as (problem, challenge, solution) triples with explicit edge types (parent-of, problem–challenge, challenge–solution). Build it automatically via SciMotivMiner, which extracts standardized, author-agnostic triples from papers and merges duplicates, then add a hierarchy using a Parent Node Addition algorithm that embeds nodes, finds nearest neighbors, and leverages an LLM to form general parent nodes. During ideation, agents use graph tools (fuzzy node search and relation retrieval) to ground ideas on concrete nodes from the graph, supplying structured, cross-linked motivational context for each proposal.

## Tie-breaking
When between two adjacent scores, default to the LOWER. Resist
optimism bias toward "novel". Score against the literature
signal only; do not apply an additional penalty when the method
reuses inspired seeds (the seeds are context, not a benchmark).

Round 1/10.

Last search results:
(empty on first round — issue your first search)

Either issue a Search (Thought + Query) or Finalize (Reasoning + Prior works + Score).

Let me first search for adversarial robustness pretraining with contrastive learning and domain adversarial alignment.

messages.content:
You are an ambitious AI researcher scoring NOVELTY of a proposed
method through iterative literature search. Be a HARSH critic — if a
search surfaces published work using the same combination on the same
problem, novelty is low.

## Definition
Novelty = the method introduces a non-trivial combination, mechanism,
or framing that has not been published in this exact form for this
problem. Surface tweaks (renamed hyperparameter, swapped backbone of
equivalent class) and well-established combinations DO NOT qualify
as novel.

## Prior-work signal
Published literature — verified via Semantic Scholar search.
Score the method against literature precedent only; the inspired
seeds shown later are context, NOT a separate scoring axis.

## Score anchors (1-5 integer)

Anchors are written so each level can be supported by what is in the
method text itself plus literature search; do NOT reward "qualitative
trade-off" claims that require running the experiment to verify.

  1 — Identical to a published technique applied to the same problem;
      a search surfaces a paper that matches the method end-to-end.
  2 — Surface variant of a published technique (renamed hyperparameter,
      swapped backbone of an equivalent class, alternate threshold);
      a search surfaces the parent technique on the same problem.
  3 — Combines 2-3 known mechanisms with prior published joint
      application to this problem family. A search surfaces at least
      one paper that pairs the same mechanisms for a closely related
      problem.
  4 — Combines mechanism families with NO surfaced published joint
      application for this problem, OR introduces one named design
      choice (a specific new component or training signal) that is
      not in the surfaced literature. Searches return prior work on
      the individual mechanisms but not their combination as
      proposed.
  5 — Introduces a new mechanism (a named component or training
      signal not present as such in any search result), AND searches
      return no DIRECT near-clone (no paper combining the same
      mechanism families for the same problem in the same
      direction). Related work that pairs ONE of the mechanisms
      with a different counterpart, or uses the same mechanisms for
      a different problem, does NOT block 5; the bar is the absence
      of an end-to-end match.

## Reference examples — 3 query domains

### Block A — "VLM visual token compression"
  Score 5: query-conditional token-merging policy via cross-attention
           scoring tokens in one pass.
  Score 4: static attention pruning + KV-cache eviction policies
           from LLM efficiency — cross-modality non-obvious pairing.
  Score 3: established attention-magnitude pruning applied to ViT
           tokens.
  Score 2: token pruning with a different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of known pruning method.

### Block B — "compositional reasoning with discrete latent structure"
  Score 5: discrete latent composition graphs induced via learned
           merge policy + RL reward shaped by downstream task.
  Score 4: VQ-VAE codes as discrete symbols + neural composition.
  Score 3: ST Gumbel-Softmax for discrete tree induction.
  Score 2: VQ-VAE with renamed quantization function.
  Score 1: standard transformer over text.

### Block C — "time-series anomaly detection in irregular sequences"
  Score 5: time-aware transformer with continuous-time positional
           encoding + downstream-cost-tuned anomaly threshold via
           RL on alarm precision/recall trade-off.
  Score 4: Neural CDE encoder + density-ratio anomaly score —
           combination not surfaced as joint application.
  Score 3: ODE-RNN encoder with reconstruction-error scoring —
           established pattern.
  Score 2: GRU autoencoder on resampled regular intervals with a
           different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of an LSTM forecasting + z-score
           baseline.

## ReAct loop — output format each round (no markdown)

Each round, output EXACTLY ONE of these two formats:

Search format (issue when more evidence is needed):
    Thought: <1-3 sentences on which mechanism pairing you want to
              verify in prior work>
    Query: <semantic scholar search keywords>

Finalize format (issue ONLY when ready to score):
    Reasoning: <2-4 sentences synthesizing what searches found and
                how it maps to a score anchor>
    Prior works: <newline-separated list of papers/methods that most
                  directly cover the proposed method, one per line
                  in the form "paper_title or method_name (year): one-line
                  summary of overlap with the proposed method".
                  Use "(none)" if score is 5 with no near-clone surfaced.>
    Score: <integer 1-5>

Hard rules:
  - DO NOT mix the search and finalize formats. If you output a
    `Query:` line, the caller treats this round as a search and
    ignores any `Score:` you write — do not waste tokens on Score
    in a search round.
  - You MUST issue at least one search before finalizing UNLESS the
    method is a trivial restatement of a known technique.
  - When you write the literal token "Score:" inside Reasoning to
    cite an anchor, write it INLINE (e.g., "matches the Score 4
    anchor"), never on its own line — the parser anchors to lines
    starting with "Score:".
  - Prior works lines must each start with "  - " (two spaces, dash,
    space) so the parser can extract them. Do NOT include a trailing
    "Score:" inside a Prior works line.

## Static context (does not change across rounds)

### Research question
Models are brittle to adversarial perturbations imperceptible to
humans; how can pretraining objectives or data augmentation make
models robust without expensive test-time defenses?


### Proposed method
Universal-Perturbation-Invariant Pretraining (UPI-Pretrain). We pretrain the backbone to make its task-relevant representation invariant to a bank of human-imperceptible perturbations, so that standard fine-tuning and inference require no test-time defenses. For each sample, we form a tuple of one clean view and several perturbed views drawn from (i) conventional stochastic augmentations, and (ii) a small, learnable universal-perturbation bank implemented as input prompts added in pixel/frequency space under an Lp and perceptual constraint. The bank contains a handful of global and class-conditional prompts; it is optimized to maximize model loss/uncertainty on a moving target backbone while remaining imperceptible, and updated with an uncertainty-guided EMA so that harder samples drive stronger prompt updates without instability.

We treat perturbations as a domain shift and factor the backbone’s pre-logit representation into z_shared (robust, task-relevant) and z_delta (nuisance, perturbation-specific). A contrastive consistency loss pulls together all views of the same sample in z_shared and pushes apart different samples. Simultaneously, a lightweight domain discriminator with a gradient-reversal layer removes perturbation information from z_shared, while an auxiliary head on z_delta predicts the perturbation type, encouraging nuisance absorption into z_delta. Classification (or label-conditioned contrastive) losses are applied only on z_shared so the decision boundary depends on perturbation-invariant features.

To further enlarge decision radii, we insert a prototype-based discriminant layer before logits. Class prototypes are learned in the shared latent space with cosine similarity and an additive angular margin; we enforce that every perturbed view remains closest to its clean-class prototype and far from others, and we add a single-pass uncertainty head trained on this embedding. During pretraining the uncertainty is only used to (i) weight hard-example losses and (ii) steer the prompt-bank EMA; at test time, the backbone runs as-is with no extra defenses.

We augment the above with a mix-and-separate pretext: randomly mix pairs of samples and their perturbations in input space (and/or feature space) and train a small separation head sharing the backbone to recover the two samples’ clean-class logits, while the main head receives standard mixup targets. This compels the representation to linearly disentangle content from nuisance perturbations and reduces reliance on brittle, high-frequency cues.

Training recipe: warm-start the backbone for a short phase with standard supervised or label-conditioned contrastive learning on clean+weakly-augmented views, then activate the perturbation bank, domain-adversarial alignment, prototype-margin loss, and mix-and-separate head. Use 3–6 prompts in the bank (1–2 global, 2–4 class-conditional), constrain their L∞ norm (e.g., ε≈2/255–4/255) and optionally low-pass filter to preserve imperceptibility. Optimize the total loss as a weighted sum of contrastive consistency, classification/proto-margin, domain-adversarial, perturbation-type prediction (on z_delta), and mix-and-separate objectives. Fine-tune on the downstream task normally; inference uses the plain backbone without test-time defenses or extra passes.

### Rationale
Training for perturbation invariance at pretraining time removes the need for expensive test-time defenses: the model has already learned to base decisions on features that are stable across a family of imperceptible attacks. A learnable universal-perturbation bank provides strong, hard augmentations without multi-step inner-loop attacks, and uncertainty-guided updates focus it where the model is fragile. Treating perturbations as a domain and explicitly factoring representations into shared vs nuisance channels, combined with gradient-reversal alignment, is an efficient way to purge attack-specific information from the decision-making subspace.

Prototype-margin constraints increase inter-class separation and enforce that all perturbed views of a sample map near the same class prototype, which widens the margin against worst-case small perturbations. Finally, a mix-and-separate auxiliary task encourages linear disentanglement of content and nuisance, reducing reliance on non-robust, high-frequency cues. Together these pretraining objectives and augmentations yield robustness with zero added cost at inference time.

### Inspired seeds (the ideation's input retrieval)
1. [IR_REC] GAG: Global Attributed Graph Neural Network for Streaming Session-based Recommendation (2007.02747)
   problem: Streaming session-based recommendation must capture short-term transitions in the current session while preserving users’ long-term preferences, but most session-based models are static, ignore user identity, or fail to adapt online.
   method:  Convert each session into a weighted directed session graph augmented with the user embedding as a global attribute, and introduce a Global Attributed Graph (GAG) neural network. GAG performs bi-directional per-edge computations conditioned on sender/receiver node features, edge weights, and the global attribute, aggregates normalized in/out neighbors to update node states, and then updates the global attribute via self-attention over item nodes conditioned on the last-clicked item and the current global attribute, with a residual connection to the user embedding. The updated global attribute becomes the session representation used to score all items. Ablations show that injecting the global attribute into both node updates and the global readout improves recommendation performance.

2. [IR_REC] Pre-training vs. Fine-tuning: A Reproducibility Study on Dense Retrieval Knowledge Acquisition (2505.07166)
   problem: Do dense retrievers acquire new retrieval knowledge during fine-tuning, or is effectiveness primarily governed by pre-trained backbones? Do prior DPR/BERT findings generalize across datasets, pooling strategies, and decoder backbones?
   method:  Conduct layer-wise linear probing by precomputing embeddings with model-specific pooling (CLS for DPR/BERT, mean for Contriever/BERT-Mean, EOS for RepLlama/Llama), then training a multiclass linear probe on concatenated query + N passage embeddings to select the relevant passage. Evaluate across Natural Questions and MS MARCO, varying N∈{2,3,4,5}, and compare fine-tuned retrievers vs their untrained backbones. Findings: For DPR (CLS), BERT-CLS often matches or outperforms DPR variants and accuracy drops as N increases, indicating pretrained knowledge underpins performance (Sec. 4.3–4.4). With mean pooling (Contriever vs BERT-Mean), the gap narrows substantially (Sec. 4.5). With a decoder backbone (RepLlama vs Llama, EOS), fine-tuning yields large late-layer gains (≈18–22% in deeper layers) (Sec. 4.6, 7), showing the DPR claim does not universally hold.

3. [LLM_NLP] Pride and Prejudice: LLM Amplifies Self-Bias in Self-Refinement (2402.11436)
   problem: LLM self-refine and self-rewarding pipelines can optimize toward falsely perceived improvements due to self-evaluation bias, causing quality to stagnate or degrade.
   method:  Empirically compare LLM self-evaluations against reference-based BLEURT and human MQM across six LLMs, four languages, and three tasks, showing universal self-bias that amplifies over iterations; in self-rewarding, increasing the candidate pool size further amplifies bias. Analyze what changes after self-refine with UniEval and style-paraphrasing experiments, finding perceived gains are mainly in fluency/understandability and style alignment rather than actual task objectives, explaining the divergence between self-estimated and true performance.

4. [CV] KOALA: A Kalman Optimization Algorithm with Loss Adaptivity (2107.03331)
   problem: Momentum improves optimization but incorporating its dynamics into a Kalman-style optimizer must remain computationally feasible for high-dimensional neural networks.
   method:  Augment the Kalman state with velocities p_k and define momentum-like dynamics x_k = x_{k-1} + p_{k-1} + w_k and p_k = κ p_{k-1} + u_k, while keeping the scalar-loss measurement h_k(x)=ˆL_k(x). Reformulate as a 2n-dimensional EKF and approximate the posterior covariance with a 2×2 block structure times identity, and approximate H_k^T H_k accordingly, yielding KOALA-M that captures momentum while retaining linear complexity in parameters. Adaptivity is further improved by online estimation of the measurement and state noise via exponential moving averages (e.g., β_R = 0.9, β_x = 0.9), reducing hyperparameter tuning and adjusting to minibatch-induced noise.

5. [ROBOTICS] Think Global, Act Local: Dual-scale Graph Transformer for Vision-and-Language Navigation (2202.11742)
   problem: Behavior cloning for sequential policies suffers from train-test distribution shift, while reinforcement learning in VLN is difficult due to sparse rewards.
   method:  DUET is first pretrained with behavior cloning and auxiliary cross-modal tasks (masked language modeling, masked region classification, single-step action prediction, and object grounding) to initialize grounded representations. It is then fine-tuned with a pseudo interactive demonstrator (PID) akin to DAgger: during rollouts, a demonstrator with access to the full environment graph selects pseudo-target actions as navigable nodes minimizing the combined distance from the current node to the node and from the node to the goal. The policy is optimized with a balance between expert and pseudo labels, yielding stronger navigation performance than RL-based fine-tuning in experiments.

6. [ROBOTICS] Multiscale Sensor Fusion and Continuous Control with Neural CDEs (2203.08715)
   problem: Continuous-time CDE policies must be causal for real-time deployment, but natural cubic spline interpolation induces non-causal dependencies on future data.
   method:  Propose a discretely-online CDE evolution: output control at a fixed high rate and evolve the CDE over successive short intervals, where at each step the cubic spline is fit only to measurements within the current interval. This preserves causality at the control frequency while retaining smooth integration of high-rate signals between sparse images. For truly continuous-time control, a zero-order-hold interpolant can be used. This strategy enables practical, real-time deployment of CDE-driven policies without violating causality.

7. [IR_REC] Efficient Pointwise-Pairwise Learning-to-Rank for News Recommendation (2409.17711)
   problem: Pairwise and listwise inference can significantly improve ranking quality but are computationally prohibitive at inference time for large candidate sets.
   method:  GLIMPSE introduces an efficient rank aggregation strategy that initializes with a pointwise-ranked list and performs right-to-left (RTL) passes that only compare adjacent items among the top-k results, swapping when the pairwise model prefers the right item. Each RTL pass over k items requires just k−1 comparisons, enabling O(K) complexity per pass versus O(K^2) for all-pairs approaches. Hyperparameters control the number of RTL passes (m) and the top-k scope, delivering practical gains with very few pairwise calls while preserving scalability.

Only prompts (~0.4% of model parameters) and the lightweight student are sent to devices, yielding consistent gains (e.g., +3.3% over pseudo-label baseline; +2.7% from U-EMA) and alleviating forgetting across continual domains.

9. [ROBOTICS] TwinTac: A Wide-Range, Highly Sensitive Tactile Sensor with Real-to-Sim Digital Twin Sensor Model (2509.10063)

   method:  PhysTac integrates eight MEMS pressure taxels (CPS135B-1500D) beneath an Ecoflex 00-31 elastomer, multiplexed over I2C (55 Hz) to achieve both ultra-high sensitivity and a wide pressure range. The sensor provides 0.01 kPa resolution over 50–1500 kPa, detects forces as low as 0.007 N and as high as 212 N, and exhibits a linear sensitivity of ~7.24 kPa/N. A radial basis function interpolation reconstructs a continuous spatial pressure map from the 8 taxels, enabling coarse shape discrimination despite the sparse array. (Supported: lines 60–75, 62–63, 138–140).

10. [RL] Inductive-bias-driven Reinforcement Learning for Efficient Schedules in Heterogeneous Clusters (1909.02119)
   problem: Adding a Bayesian model to guide RL typically requires full probabilistic inference during training, which is computationally prohibitive for periodic retraining in real systems.
   method:  The paper introduces a sampling-based gradient estimator for Bayesian networks that computes gradients of BN parameters without performing full posterior inference. By generating samples and expanding only the necessary BN subgraph, it recursively estimates gradients with favorable complexity (especially on polytree BNs), decoupling BN training from the inference procedure used to form the belief state. This accelerates learning, yielding about 4× faster training than belief-propagation-based full inference and ~2.2× better sample efficiency than a model-free RNN baseline.

   problem: Downstream video understanding and selection tasks lack temporally fine-grained supervision to pick informative frames and reason about ordered events.
   method:  Use ProgressCaptioner’s per-frame, progress-aware captions as an intermediate textual representation. An LLM compares consecutive captions to select keyframes that mark action progression, improving action recognition when replacing uniformly sampled frames. The same captions guide zero-shot frame-wise classification (via LLM multi-choice over labels) and enhance zero-shot video QA on NExT-QA (ATP-Hard), surpassing prior zero-shot baselines and improving temporal reasoning performance.

   method:  The paper proposes a textile taxonomy that jointly considers fiber material (e.g., wool, polyester, cotton) and production method (woven vs. knitted).
